data.title: Ed Miliband plots crackdown on wind farms ‘gaming’ power system
data.description:
Wind farm owners who earn huge payouts from gaming Britain’s energy system face a crackdown under new reforms proposed by power grid bosses.

The National Energy System Operator (Neso) warned on Wednesday that companies were deliberately clogging up Britain’s energy system to boost their own revenues.

This is because wind farms can be paid to switch off when the grid becomes too congested to transport their power, allowing traders to game the system.

Neso, which handles the day-to-day operations of the electricity grid, said it was concerned about the sale of power generated by Scottish wind farms to buyers in the South, even when traders know that grid congestion means the power physically cannot be delivered.

There is currently no penalty for doing this, but it means Neso must constantly intervene to balance the system.

For example, it will pay Scottish wind farms to switch off while also paying another power station in the South to generate replacement electricity.

Under the current model, the wind farm and the power station could also be owned by the same company, allowing that company to distort supply to earn payments.

Neso warned the system had created an incentive for companies “to aggravate the constraint” as this “maximises the market participant’s expected revenue”.

It now wants to force generators to sell power from individual power stations, making it more obvious when the system is being gamed.

It also wants to require generators to settle their plans further in advance to minimise last-minute changes to the system.

Balancing costs, which can easily amount to millions of pounds per day, are ultimately passed on to households through their bills.

The problem threatens to push up household bills in a blow to Ed Miliband’s promise to cut them by £300 a year.

Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, last year scrapped reforms that supporters said would have ended such market gaming practices following an intense lobbying campaign by wind farm owners, who argued it would torpedo investment.

The amount spent on balancing the grid hit £2.7bn last year alone and is expected to reach between £5bn and £8bn a year by 2030 without action to tackle the problem, according to Neso.

Neso warned: “The misalignment between the national wholesale price and locational balancing needs, along with the scale and predictability of congestion, presents significant risks of strategic positioning against constraints.

“This risk is more likely to arise if the network becomes more congested.”

RenewableUK, which represents wind farm owners, claimed the problem affected “all types of power generation”.

A Neso report last year said most constraint costs were focused in Scotland, where Britain’s wind farms are most concentrated.

A RenewableUK spokesman added: “The industry is working closely with the Government to ensure that innovative solutions are rolled out at speed, such as new energy storage capacity, to add more flexibility into the system”.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “After years of historic underinvestment, we are delivering the biggest upgrade in Great Britain’s electricity network in decades to minimise constraint costs, delivering clean power by 2030 and helping bring down bills for good.

“We are working closely with Neso, Ofgem and industry to further improve the system as part of our Reformed National Pricing package.”

data.title: Starmer is putting his political survival ahead of the national interest
data.description:
Back in 2015, during the so-called “golden era” of Sino-British relations, Xi Jinping was welcomed to Britain for a full state visit.

Between the pomp and ceremony, he was taken to the University of Manchester facility where graphene – an extremely strong and thin material – had been invented.

The Government wanted to showcase to Xi what we were capable of and persuade him to open China’s huge markets to our products. By all accounts, Xi left impressed.Partnerships with China’s Engine Aero Corporation and Central South University soon followed.

The problem, however, was that he left far too impressed. Years later, it emerged that Beijing had sent a student to study at our National Graphene Institute. After finishing his PhD, he transferred to Beijing’s National University of Defence Technology and worked on key projects for the Chinese military. Soon after, China’s latest attack helicopters were fitted with graphene armour.

The naivety ended up backfiring economically too. Although graphene was invented in Britain, fewer than 2 per cent of graphene-related patents are registered here today. Around 60 per cent are registered in China.

The idea that engagement would influence China in a more positive direction was no less misguided. On his visit Xi was gifted a box set of Sherlock, then a firm British favourite on Chinese TV, and sunk pints at the Plough pub near Chequers.

But the fake bonhomie proved utterly pointless. The Golden Era was followed by oppression in Hong Kong, mass incarceration in Xinjiang and massive increases in military activity around Taiwan.

Starmer knows the dark reality of our relationship with China better than anybody. He will be regularly briefed by our security agencies on their industrial-scale espionage. He will have read the reports on their currency manipulation, dumping and intellectual property theft. And he has seen the way China weaponises economic integration to bully our allies who refuse to submit to them. When Australia rightly called for an investigation into the origins of Covid-19, Beijing slapped them with crippling tariffs.

So, why, given all this, has Starmer gone cap in hand to Beijing? The short answer is he is desperate.

His blunt calculation is that he needs their money. And he needs it now. The Chancellor’s coffers are empty. Growth has ground to a halt. He’s after some private investment that might inch up his meagre growth figures by a few decimal points.

Fighting for his personal survival, he cares not one jot about the long-term consequences.

Undermined our Armed Forces

Of course, improving his own lot at the expense of his country’s long-term security is nothing new for this Prime Minister. His legal career was founded upon so-called human rights claims that undermined our Armed Forces. He claims he was obliged to accept such work but as The Telegraph revealed this week, he worked with the disgraced solicitor Phil Shiner for free.

His work “challeng[ing] the government over the reach of its anti-terror legislation and taken on the British military”, according to The Guardian, led to his appointment as Director of Public Prosecutions and subsequent political career.

The question is, will Starmer be allowed to repeat the trick of enhancing his own career at the expense of his country’s interests?

To boost our country’s growth and living standards, the Prime Minister doesn’t even need to fly to Beijing to offer himself as tribute.

One of the fallacies of globalisation was that manufacturing could be outsourced to the East, but Britain could remain a powerhouse of innovation. The idea was that companies like Dyson could make their products overseas, but bright sparks here could continue to design them all thousands of miles from the production line. It doesn’t work like that. The two are too inextricably linked, as every Western economy has now learnt to its cost.

Starmer needs what Nigel Farage and Reform are championing: reindustrialisation. Since 2003 our heavy industry has halved. It’s left our economy weak, lopsided and vulnerable. It’s this industrial malaise that underlies much of our decline.

The maladies behind it are well rehearsed in these pages: the highest energy prices in the developed world, sclerotic planning, and insane net zero targets to name but a few. Ending these drag anchors on our economy and returning to making and building things would require the Prime Minister to stand up to those he fears far more than a dictator; Ed Miliband and his backbenchers.

The path back to growth lies not in Manchurian capitulation but Mancunian innovation. We still have some of the most innovative and entrepreneurial people in the world. We just need a Government that will see them, not our adversaries, as the key to our future.

data.title: Ocado’s dream of being the Tesla of groceries is unravelling fast
data.description:
When Tim Steiner retires, he would no doubt like to be remembered as Britain’s Elon Musk. After all, it was only recently that the Ocado founder was still breezily comparing the grocery outfit to Tesla.

There is no doubting Steiner’s entrepreneurial vision. But sadly, he increasingly risks being remembered as little more than a very good salesman.

Over the last 25 years, the founder of Ocado has repeatedly convinced some of the supposedly smartest people in the City and on Wall Street that his company is on the cusp of changing the face of supermarket shopping for good.

He has repeated this feat time and time again – persuading shareholders, bankers and the bond markets to lend Ocado vast sums of money to fulfil his dream of bringing online groceries to the masses.

If there was some sign of this coming true, you could perhaps understand their eagerness.

Since its inception, it is estimated that Ocado has raised more than £5.5bn – nearly three times its current stock-market value – from the equity and debt markets. Yet, when you consider that this is a business that has racked up £1.8bn of losses since going public in 2010, the enthusiasm is baffling

Perhaps the news that Ocado is planning as many as 1,000 job cuts as part of a scramble to rein in costs will convince the die-hards that Steiner’s “jam-tomorrow” story is destined to remain so forever.

The company has said little in response to reports of the cuts, other than that it regularly reviews its operations “to ensure we’re set up for long-term success,” – which is just silly. Ditto the idea that firing hundreds of people is somehow a demonstration of strength.

Instead, it smacks of an organisation running out of ideas and, potentially road. The truth is Ocado is in another spin, possibly its biggest yet, after several major customers watered down their ties with the Hatfield-based company.

Against that grim backdrop, all eyes are on Ocado’s annual results in a fortnight’s time after a 40pc slump in its share price in the last six months.

For Steiner to retain whatever credibility he has left after the disastrous events of recent months, the company needs to honour its promise to turn cash flow positive in the next financial year.

Its evangelical founder has said this is Ocado’s “core priority”, and full-year profitability could come shortly.

Yet with key customers scaling back their partnerships, it is undeniable that there is suddenly a sharper focus on what insiders describe as a commitment to “rigorous cost and capital discipline”.

Bernstein analyst William Woods predicted last month that there will now need to be “material cost-cutting” to the workforce, as well as the capital to invest in its supposedly world-beating technology.

At the same time, he pared his target share price to 100p, which is 58pc below the 229p level at which the stock closed on Monday.

Steiner’s grand vision has long been for Ocado to evolve from an online grocery delivery service to a technology provider that builds everything from consumer-facing websites to robot-operated depots for other food retailers – “the Tesla of grocery”, as he called it as recently as 2023.

It’s an intoxicating story that goes a long way to explaining why sophisticated institutional shareholders such as the hedge fund Roditi and money giant Schroders have been backers from day one.

However, the inconvenient truth that Steiner and his acolytes continue to overlook is that grocery deliveries remain unprofitable.

This is particularly true of the sort of hi-tech wizardry that Ocado provides.

The biggest problem facing the company is the vast amounts of upfront capital required to roll out large, robot-operated warehouses, the accompanying software and fleets of refrigerated vans – hence the need to repeatedly raise large sums of money from investors and the debt markets.

It is a model built on the premise that such outlays would eventually be recovered through lucrative long-term contracts with the biggest supermarkets worldwide, who would be happy to pay hefty licensing and consultancy fees for the privilege of using Ocado’s state-of-the-art technology.

For a while, it seemed a plausible scenario. Never more so than during the pandemic, when Steiner declared that “a dramatic and permanent shift towards online grocery shopping” was underway.

It was a boast that helped propel Ocado’s share price to the nosebleed heights of £29, and at one stage left the company worth more than double Sainsbury’s and Morrisons combined.

For now, management can still point to a long list of major customers, including Coles in Australia, Casino Group in France and ICA in Sweden. But for how long?

Kroger’s bombshell decision in November to close three of eight Ocado warehouses in the US was followed in January by an announcement from Canada’s Sobeys that it would close a distribution centre using Ocado’s kit.

The Kroger pullback on its own threatens to be pretty devastating to Ocado’s prospects.

The US chain is its most valuable customer by a long way – “not just half the embedded value, probably half the future value” – in the words of one retail chief. And its claim that the Ocado set-up only works in “higher-density” areas suggests serious limitations to the expansionary story it has been selling for years.

Ever the optimist, Steiner has attempted to spin the setback as an opportunity.

Under the Kroger tie-up, Ocado was prevented from doing deals with its rivals in the US and other international markets. Therefore, it is now free to seek other partners, the logic goes.

But with Sobeys quickly struck by buyer’s remorse too, surely the overwhelming question isn’t how many new customers Ocado can bring in but how many of its existing customers it can hold onto?

Steiner may be putting on a brave face, but a warning from Kroger that it will be keeping tabs on results at the remaining Ocado facilities must be giving him sleepless nights.

If his biggest customer is struggling to make the economics work, then surely the rest are?

data.title: Chinese EVs will destroy jobs unless Government acts, supplier warns
data.description:
European nations risk losing millions of jobs unless they stem the flood of cheap electric cars from China, the new owner of UK car parts supplier Dowlais has warned.

David Dauch, the chief executive of US-based Dauch Corp, said that an influx of electric vehicles (EVs) from the Far East threatens to wipe out vast swathes of suppliers in the West.

He said: “Countries are going to have to put the right policies in place to make sure it’s a level playing field for the suppliers and manufacturers in each of those countries.

“If not, then they run the risk of losing jobs and potentially losing industries.”

Europe’s automotive industry employs about 14m people across carmakers and suppliers.

Mr Dauch said Europe will have to consider whether to follow Donald Trump in effectively blocking Chinese EVs from the US through a combination of 100pc tariffs and strict regulations.

He said: “Right now, they are banned from coming into the US because of national security and other reasons.

“The US has put certain things in place, tariffs or other measures, to prevent them from coming in because we’re trying to protect the manufacturing base and jobs in our country.

“Other countries have to assess that on an individual basis and make a decision on what’s best for their citizens and the GDPs in each of their economies.”

Almost 28pc of the EVs sold in Britain last year were Chinese following an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda.

Dauch Corp’s £1.16bn purchase of GKN Automotive’s holding company, Dowlais, will create a global leader in components that transmit power to a car’s wheels such as driveshafts, differentials and axles.

The US firm, which changed its name from American Axle last month, caters mostly to the internal combustion engine vehicles that remain dominant in North America.

GKN – which will be known by its original name following the abolition of the Dowlais brand – is also a leader in EV drive systems.

Mr Dauch said the enlarged firm will adopt an “agnostic” stance on EVs versus traditional vehicles, claiming that the rush to move on from petrol and diesel engines had been overdone.

He said: “Several countries have tried to move too fast when they weren’t properly set up to support that.

“I think the UK was one of those and the US was one of those. Things were politically motivated. There are certain ways that people are trying to drive policy.

“Many countries have already gone back to review their initial policies, several have already made a decision to push those out, others have not. I think it’s going to be dependent on what the impact is on the manufacturing base for each country.”

Mr Dauch said that the Trump administration’s elimination of EV incentives had caused its market share fall from a peak of 10pc to around 5pc.

He said that makes it extremely unlikely that battery, plug-in hybrid and fuel-cell vehicles will capture 50pc of US sales by 2030, as targeted by Joe Biden, the former US president.

“I’m not saying it won’t happen over time but the time is going to be much longer than people think,” he said.

Mr Dauch said that while EVs are doing better in Europe, expecting the average driver to switch to models that cost more and have a limited range and insufficient charging infrastructure was to deny market forces.

He said: “The consumer is the boss and the only way they are going to buy a vehicle is if it’s affordable, safe and they can charge it.”

The Dowlais name was adopted in 2023 when GKN Automotive was spun off from GKN Aerospace by owner Melrose.

data.title: The auto industry’s gamble on electric cars has turned into a catastrophe
data.description:
We are already getting close to the €100bn (£87bn) mark and the total is still rising.

Stellantis – the company that controls Fiat, Peugeot and Chrysler – announced on Friday that it was writing down €22bn on its electric vehicle unit. The bill is only the latest in a series of enormous losses suffered by the major American and European auto manufacturers who jumped on the EV bandwagon.

In reality, it turns out that electric cars are only a small part of the overall market and that, insofar as it exists, Chinese manufacturers will capture most of the sales.

The gamble on electric cars has turned into a catastrophe and it will be many years before the industry recovers.

With less than four years remaining until the original target date for banning the sale of all new petrol and diesel cars, the giants of the industry were meant to be riding a boom in sales of battery-powered vehicles by now.

Sleek new models would be rolling off the production lines, new battery plants would be creating hundreds of thousands of jobs, while the billions poured into investment would be the catalyst for reindustrialising both Europe and the United States.

“We’re going to need 70,000 skilled people just to make batteries across this country,” announced Boris Johnson, the former prime minister, back in 2021. He promised unlimited government support for British EV production.

Over in France, Emmanuel Macron, the French president, was pouring billions into making his country a force in battery and EV production.

So we are starting to see the results of all that investment, right? Sales are booming, profits are rising and new jobs are being created? Well, not exactly.

Stellantis’s €22bn write-down on Friday, combined with plans to offload a stake in a Canadian battery joint venture, sent shares down 27pc on the day. They have now more than halved over the last year, taking Stellantis’s value down to just €17bn.

Unfortunately, it is far from alone. General Motors said last month that it was taking a $7bn (£5bn) charge against earnings, almost all of which was accounted for by its EV unit.

Just before Christmas, Ford said it was taking a $19.5bn hit on its EV business, including cancelled vehicles and a battery joint venture. Volkswagen has started scaling back production of EVs amid heavy losses.

Porsche, which had said that electric vehicles would account for 80pc of profits by the end of this decade, instead announced a €3.1bn charge late last year after scaling back those targets.

Even Tesla, a company that knows more about electric cars than any of its rivals, is starting to struggle. It has scrapped the iconic Model S and Elon Musk, its chief executive, is desperately trying to convince everyone that robots are the future instead.

Antonio Filosa, the chief executive of Stellantis, conceded that the company had overestimated “the pace of the energy transition that distanced us from many car buyers’ real-world needs, means and desires”.

It is a painful admission but one that is at least honest. One point is surely clear. We are not hearing very much about how the transition to EVs would lead to an industrial renaissance any more.

There have been two major problems. First, EVs may only be a niche product.

Drivers are worried about the range, it is far from clear they are better for the environment once the impact of all the raw materials in the manufacturing process is taken into account, the charging infrastructure is not in place and we don’t generate the electricity to power them all at a price cheap enough to make EVs cost-effective.

Next, where there is a market, the new breed of Chinese brands led by BYD is walking away with it.

The traditional auto giants thought the transition was just a matter of replacing an internal combustion engine with a big battery, but it turned out that an EV was a piece of electronics with wheels attached. It has much more in common with the mobile phone market than anything the petrolheads running the industry were familiar with.

It is far easier to create a new EV company from scratch than to convert one of the traditional giants.

The result? All the auto companies are now in deep trouble.

Even worse, under the direction of Ed Miliband, the fanatical Energy Secretary, Britain is pressing on blindly with the 2030 target for phasing out sales of new petrol cars even as the rest of the world recognises that it is complete madness.

Indeed, only this week, the boss of South Korea’s Hyundai warned that sticking to the target threatened “severe disruption” in the UK auto market.

The blunt truth is this: the massive bet that the auto giants took on EVs has backfired spectacularly.

It has cost them tens of billions in capital, it has wasted five years of time and energy that could have been more profitably deployed elsewhere and – perhaps worst of all – it has allowed Chinese manufacturers that no one had ever heard of to establish new brands. It will very difficult to get rid of them now.

It is one of the worst self-inflicted calamities of recent industrial history.

To start the recovery process, the manufacturers will have to persuade governments to stop imposing ridiculous top-down targets that only distort the market. They will have to ruthlessly get rid of units that don’t make any money. They will have to wean themselves off subsidies and, most crucially, they will need to reconnect with their customers, making cars that people can afford and want to drive again.

After the madness of the last five years, it is the only way back – and it will be a long, hard and expensive road.

data.title: Independent takeover of The Standard faces delays after board exodus
data.description:
The Independent’s proposed takeover of The Standard is facing delays after a boardroom clear-out plunged the London newspaper into chaos.

The Telegraph has learnt that Tamar Riley has resigned as chief executive of The Standard after just seven months in the role. Her exit follows the departure of Anna van Praagh, the company’s chief content officer, earlier this week.

The changes have taken place ahead of The Independent’s planned deal to take over the running of The Standard’s website and commercial operations. The deal was announced three weeks ago but completion is now poised to be pushed back.

The transfer was initially planned for March 1, with journalists told they could apply for voluntary redundancy before that date. Anyone who refused to transfer would have their contracts terminated.

However, bosses have acknowledged that the transfer is now unlikely to take place next month as planned.

One senior insider said there were “new levels of chaos” at The Standard, which shut down its daily print operations and moved to a weekly edition in late 2024.

Those inside the newsroom now fear that The Standard’s remaining digital operations could be shut down if the deal falls apart.

A source close to The Independent insisted that the executive departures would not be a barrier to negotiations, nor to the success of the deal.

Staff were already in uproar over plans to combine the two titles, which are both backed by Lord Lebedev.

Despite the takeover, The Standard said it would maintain a “core” team of around 25 people.

The abrupt exit of The Standard’s two most senior executives underscores the recent turmoil at the London freesheet amid declining readership.

The rise of smartphones, flexible working and Wi-Fi on the London Underground have all dented reader numbers and sparked heavy losses, forcing the newspaper to shutter its daily edition.

The restructuring last year led to around 150 job losses including at least 70 cuts in editorial, slashing the newsroom by more than half.

The Standard has also been without a permanent editor since the overhaul, when Dylan Jones stepped down after just 18 months in charge.

Meanwhile, critics have questioned the purpose of the new weekly edition, which is more focused on arts and culture than on London news.

The publisher’s website has also shifted its focus to social media-friendly celebrity news – a change in direction that some fear will only accelerate under The Independent.

Ms Riley took over as chief executive of The Standard in July, having previously led female-focused digital media brand Refinery29.

Ms van Praagh is an experienced journalist who has worked at The Standard for more than a decade.

She was expected to oversee the transition to The Independent but raised eyebrows last month when she launched her own newsletter on Substack.

The Standard has been contacted for comment.

data.title: Bring back the great British TV drama of whisky-swilling businessmen
data.description:
The way we view television has changed enormously in the 73 years since Queen Elizabeth II’s coronation. We’ve gone from flickering black-and-white boxes that took a good 30 seconds to warm up and served up just one channel, to widescreen OLED smart devices that stream thousands of choices of “content” (though, ironically, they still often take a good 30 seconds to start up).

But the actual shows? Although they look very different, the genres of TV drama have stayed remarkably steady over all that time. Flick through the TV listings from any year over the decades, and you will find crime shows, medical shows, soaps, sci-fi, costume drama, historical series, and so on. There’s not all that great a gap between Emergency Ward 10 and Casualty. There’s only one kind of drama series that you will find in a 1966 edition of TV Times that you won’t see while scrolling through your EPG in 2026 (excluding westerns, which were obviously never a British concern anyway). I’m talking about business TV.

There used to be many of these series: The Brothers, The Power Game, The Plane Makers, Mogul, The Troubleshooters. These were gripping stories of men (and it was overwhelmingly men) striding into each others’ offices, pouring whiskies, sparking up cigars, and obsessing over boards, stocks and shares, new products and processes. Women did feature, but until the Eighties they were the wives and secretaries that orbited like satellites around the boardrooms and factory floors where the main events played out.

Now, I will hear you object that boardroom business drama survives – Succession or Industry, for example. The big difference between these modern shows and their forebears is that they aren’t really concerned with the actual business of the business, except where it directly affects the drama between the characters.

Succession didn’t have a storyline about the design of a product – you can’t imagine scenes with Shiv or Roman unfurling plans across a big teak table, or lengthy discussions about the pros and cons of the fuel injection nozzle or whatever. No, Succession is more the inheritor of the Dallas model, the high-glamour soap in which the actual work in hand is merely the convenient backdrop.

The true business TV drama of old portrayed the actual business as dramatic in itself. The heyday of the form was 1963’s The Plane Makers, the story of an aviation design firm which after three series reinvented itself in 1965 as The Power Game, shifting its anti-hero John Wilder (Patrick Wymark) to a merchant bank. The tensions of manufacture, negotiations and industrial relations were depicted in detail that seems baffling today.

There is a sense of great public interest in business in other shows of the time. Early Honor Blackman episodes of The Avengers feature Steed and Mrs Gale investigating chicanery at works making pottery and optical lenses, and trying to expose export licences for counterfeit packaging. It’s all very unusual to 21st-century eyes.

The Wymark model – the tycoon stomping about, all blood and thunder, in his executive suite – became such an established part of TV that Fry and Laurie would spoof it in their John and Peter sketches: “Damn it to damnation, triple blast, John, I want to know what the hell is going on!”

The sometimes strained and phoney scene-setting dialogue of the genre – as we pick up the characters before the meat of the scene – was also brilliantly lampooned by Victoria Wood, who opens a visit to Acorn Antiques with Miss Babs pointing at blank sheets of paper and telling Miss Berta, “Yes, that’s much better – if we deliver these antiques on Friday morning, we can take delivery of these antiques in the afternoon.”

By the Seventies, the genre had become so established that it started generating oddities of its own. Hine, from 1971, is a fascinatingly weird one-off, created by Power Game writer Wilfred Greatorex (what a fantastic “Dammit, Marjorie!” name that is).

Hine starred the personable Barrie Ingham as the titular hero, who is a freelance international arms dealer. Viewers were baffled – were we really meant to be rooting for this charming chap in his attempts to sell British weapons to dictatorships in the Middle East? – and the show flopped. It stands now as a fascinatingly amoral glimpse into a hidden world.

The episode “The Old School Noose” by Arden Winch (another great moniker and nothing to do with the workwear firm of the same name), in which Hine is caught in an elaborate web involving his childhood best friend who is now a Labour MP, is one of those exquisite pieces of dramatic writing and acting that occasionally startles you in a vintage “hack-for-hire” series.

So what became of the “pure” business drama? The last British example I can think of that still retained elements of it was Eighties super-soap Howards’ Way, the BBC’s answer to Dynasty. Much of the action in its initial stages revolved around the design and production of a new racing boat, and the dilemma between using hand-crafted or machine-made mouldings. Try pitching that to Netflix. But fairly soon this faded into the background, swallowed up by the ins and outs of Relton Marine, Leisurecruise, the House of Howard, and which character owned what percentage of them.

In fact, in the Nineties, business started infecting our less glam soaps. When I worked as a writer on Coronation Street, Fiona Middleton – aged 21! – owned the salon, while Brookside’s teenage entrepreneur Jacqui Dixon had fingers in investment pies everywhere, and in EastEnders Sharon Watts became Walford’s answer to Howard Hughes. These may have been intended as inspiring messages of female empowerment, but they were both highly unlikely and difficult for viewers to identify with.

The simple answer to the mystery of business TV’s decline is that Britain stopped manufacturing things. There are currently politicians on all sides mooting the return of long-defunct industries to our land. In the unlikely event that they succeed, perhaps once again viewers will see people slamming down tumblers of Scotch and bellowing at their secretaries, damn it!

data.title: Labour’s property market plot could hammer your house price
data.description:
When Harold Wilson launched Labour’s housebuilding blitz in 1964, he hoped to tap into Britain’s wartime spirit of two decades earlier to unleash a British building renaissance.

“Just as in the war, the idea of a common purpose had a dynamising effect, so possibly the launching of a great housing plan could have a similar effect today,” he told Labour members.

Wilson’s Labour government went on to phenomenal success, building over 400,000 homes a year by 1968 – a UK record which is still to be surpassed.

Almost 60 years on and Labour’s latest crop of ministers are wrestling with a similar housing crisis.

They have pledged to build 300,000 homes a year while in power.

But instead of Wilson’s soaring rhetoric, ministers are pushing a more puzzling message to galvanise the public: to help more young people get on the property ladder, we are going to hammer the price of your house to help make things fairer.

Last week, Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, admitted that house prices will fall if Labour meets its target to build 1.5 million homes this parliament.

“Our analysis, in broad terms, estimates that a 1pc increase in housing stock should … decrease prices by 2pc,” Pennycook told a Commons committee on Tuesday.

Pennycook, a 43-year-old former think tank academic from west London, said more housebuilding would lead to a “sustained levelling-off of house price growth, and then a gradual easing of it over time”.

It is an aim that may resonate with young people locked out of the property market.

But for swathes of British homeowners, it has sparked fears that Labour is now coming for their property nest eggs.

Property is an obsession in this country because it comprises 40pc of household wealth in the UK – with much of that going towards funding retirement.

“People who paid off their mortgages and have got an awful lot of equity tied up in their housing are protective of the price of their houses,” says Paul Cheshire, a professor at the London School of Economics.

“There is a tension between what you might call the housing ‘haves’ and the housing ‘have-nots’. The balance is moving towards the housing have-nots.”

In a bid to calm fears, Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, was rushed out after Pennycook’s appearance to dispel the idea that Labour wanted to hurt house prices.

“We don’t want house prices to come down where people own their own home,” he said.

“We need to build more homes so it’s more affordable for young people in particular to get that first foot on the housing ladder.”

Yet the alarming messaging around the plan has prompted further scrutiny on Labour’s building policy.

The party came to power in 2024 promising a housing revolution.

Guided by Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister, it said 1.5 million homes would be built by 2030 – about 300,000 a year.

Today, Labour claims it has started 310,000 homes since it came into power, equalling an average monthly rate of 17,200.

This leaves 1.2 million to finish in the remaining 42 months until July 2029 – meaning they need to build 28,000 per month.

Yet, according to Tom Bill, head of UK residential research at Knight Frank, attempts to bring down house prices by increasing supply “seems like a simple economic truth but [it] fundamentally misunderstands how the sector operates”.

“Housebuilders deliver homes at a rate and price that is sustainable for their business, not because it’s written down in a political manifesto,” he says.

“The whole development process needs to be smoother and affordability is a genuine concern in some areas, but this latest intervention is like asking a car manufacturer to speed up its production line in order to sell cheaper hatchbacks,” Bill adds.

Britain has faced a severe shortage of housing for decades. Since 2000, house prices have risen by nearly 250pc, locking many out of the market and forcing them to rent or live under their parents’ roofs.

For Pennycook, much of the blame for this is on the housebuilders and the property industry.

“People in London that bought land at the top of the market for too high a price and are now sitting on it. And there will probably need to be a market adjustment in London,” he told the Financial Times last month.

Tim Leunig, a former adviser to Rishi Sunak, called Pennycook’s objective a “sensible aspiration” but doubted Labour could make it stick.

“If prices fell by 1pc or 2pc a year for a while that would be great,” he said. “Add in inflation … and real wage growth … and you are looking at a 5pc improvement in affordability a year, or close to 25pc in a parliament.

“That would be transformational for a lot of people. Sadly [Labour’s] policies are unlikely to deliver that.”

Builders have also disputed Pennycook’s diagnosis of the problem, saying his claims are “bonkers”.

Graham Prothero, chief executive of housebuilder MJ Gleeson, says: “There appears to be a bit of an ideology that says, if developers didn’t ask for such high profits, then houses would be cheap and everything would be wonderful. It cannot be that simple.”

He adds that he was “very disappointed” at an implication that prices are high because developers sit on land so that it can gain more value over time.

“If my business is to make money out of selling houses, why on earth would I hold them back on the basis that I might make speculative profit out of a green field?” says Prothero.

“It is absolutely bonkers. The moment I buy that site, it’s costing me money. What I want to do is get it through planning and sell the houses, then I can start to close off my risk and actually hopefully sell [at] a profit.”

Politically, the mixed signals coming from the housing department also underscore an ideological divide between the different factions of the Labour Party.

With the likes of Andy Burnham and Ed Miliband applying more pressure on Sir Keir to lean Left after swerving last week’s leadership challenge, Pennycook’s stance is likely to be interpreted as a way for Sir Keir to score some political points.

Many voters have children who may be stuck at home and need help with funding their deposits for a place of their own.

But it could alienate the share of Labour voters who own properties and have swung towards the party away from the Conservatives in recent years.

In the General Election, Labour secured a 17-point lead among mortgage holders and reduced the Tories’ advantage with outright homeowners from 35 points to just 10 points, an Ipsos poll found.

Labour has largely pinned its housebuilding ambitions on bringing in planning reforms to ease the regulatory burden on property developers.

London, which underpins much of Labour’s housebuilding target, remains a fundamental concern for many in the industry, given it remains hugely expensive relative to people’s incomes.

Housebuilding has slumped by 84pc over the past decade, according to Molior, with construction starting on just 5,547 private homes in 2025.

“In London, we are nowhere near building enough to keep pace with half of what demand is, let alone outpace demand and bring prices down,” says Sam Dumitriu, the head of policy at the think tank Britain Remade.

‘Bad deal’ for builders

Building thousands of new homes every year also has implications if they are marketed incorrectly.

Dumitriu points out that putting every single home on a newly built estate on the market at once would lead to “quite a lot of vacancy” and create potential for negative price impacts.

“You might say that it’s a good deal for consumers, a bad deal for the builder, but fundamentally the builder has to factor that in when they build it,” he says.

Labour and Pennycook are due to set out a new housing strategy in the coming months.

With Labour’s last building blitz in the 1960s a distant memory, Sir Keir and his colleagues will need to find their own wartime spirit to achieve their aims.

A spokesman at the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “We are fixing the housing crisis we inherited by accelerating reforms to get Britain building and restore the dream of home ownership.”

The spokesman added: “Major planning changes are already under way and we’re investing £39bn in social and affordable housing.

“Our landmark plan for new towns across the country will also create more attractive, well-designed places where people want to live.”

data.title: Boohoo tycoon must have known about Leicester sweatshops, lawyers claim
data.description:
The co-founder of Boohoo is likely to have known about the “terrible, unsafe and unsanitary conditions” in Leicester sweatshops that supplied his business before the scandal came to light, a High Court lawsuit has claimed.

Mahmud Kamani, who started the online clothes seller in 2006, was probably aware that the factories Boohoo bought its clothes from were underpaying workers and subjecting them to “arbitrary abuse and humiliation,” lawyers claimed.

“It is not, the claimants say, credible that Mr Kamani was unaware of the conditions in which those factories were being operated,” the lawsuit says.

“Boohoo denies the alleged knowledge of its board. The claimants say that these denials are not convincing.”

The claim emerged in a lawsuit in which investors are seeking £177m plus interest from Boohoo in compensation for a share price crash in 2020.

Boohoo’s stock fell by 42pc after press reports revealed workers at Leicester factories that supply it were paid “significantly less than the minimum wage” with many on as little as £3 per hour. Staff were also working long hours in cramped conditions and dilapidated buildings.

City law firm Fox Williams, representing the investors, claims Mr Kamani, Boohoo’s executive chairman, had long-standing relationships with the owners of the Leicester sweatshops, attended social events with some of the suppliers and even attended some of the factories himself.

Lawyers are now pushing for Mr Kamani to hand over logs of his communications with two of his sons, Umar and Samir, on the basis that those messages likely contain relevant information.

The skeleton argument says Mr Kamani “is highly likely to have discussed relevant matters with his sons,” while noting both Umar and Samir Kamani held positions in the company as the heads of major brands.

Labour rights activists claimed that factory staff were also expected to come into work while sick with Covid-19 at the height of the pandemic, when Leicester was under stringent lockdown restrictions.

Controversy wiped £1bn off value

The controversy wiped more than £1bn off of Boohoo’s value following the plunge in the company’s share price on London’s Alternative Investment Market. Boohoo subsequently launched a £55m takeover of Debenhams in 2021 and rebranded under the British department store’s name.

The lawsuit says Boohoo failed to “take its social responsibility seriously” and “did not strive for high standards in ethics throughout its supply chain,” prior to the scandal being unveiled.

It points out that the wider Kamani family also held 37pc of all shares in Boohoo, while pointing to claims the listed business had “vestiges and traits of a family business.”

It says that the Kamani family had links to some of the Leicester factories and that some of the suppliers were even introduced to Boohoo by Mr Kamani’s brother, Jalal, the company’s former trading director.

In response, lawyers for Boohoo, from Herbert Smith Freehills, said: “There is no reasonable basis to include members of the Kamani family other than Mahmud Kamani,” while noting none of them held senior leadership positions inside the fast-fashion retailer.

Boohoo said it “strongly contests the allegations and will vigorously defend any claim.”

The investors include the California State Teachers’ Retirement System, or CalSTRS, which manages over $390bn (£285bn) worth of pensions.

data.title: AI paranoia grips the City as chatbots take over
data.description:
Amadeo Alentorn still remembers the nervous questions he was asked when he began to let computer algorithms dictate stock market trades more than 20 years ago.

“Some investors would come to us and say, ‘What? You let computers decide which stocks to buy?’” says Alentorn, the head of systematic equities at Jupiter Asset Management.

“We said, ‘Yes, that’s exactly what we do. We build investment strategies that get coded up and then computers make those decisions.’”

Algorithmic trading has become the norm today for asset managers and other professional investors. But computers could soon be taking over even more of our financial lives.

Billions have been wiped off the market value of wealth management stocks this week as investors panic that artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots and programmes will soon be able to manage people’s money.

The sector is the latest to be hit by an AI panic – with software, law and accounting all suffering similar crashes in recent days.

More than £1bn has been wiped off the value of St James’s Place, the biggest wealth manager in Britain, this week, while rivals such as Quilter also tumbled. Stockbroking platforms have also crashed, with AJ Bell also falling.

The latest turmoil was triggered on Tuesday, when Altruist, a “unicorn” Los Angeles start-up, put out a press release announcing the launch of a new AI tax-strategy planner.

The programme, called Hazel, can write personalised tax strategies in minutes and can run interactive modelling on everything from the impact of a bonus to a home sale or retirement.

Jason Wenk, Altruist’s founder, says it “expands what a single advisor can handle, raises the bar on outcomes, and makes average advice a lot harder to justify”.

The tool has triggered mass panic in the stock market.

In the US, shares in wealth management and investment companies Charles Schwab, Raymond James and Morgan Stanley all tumbled. European equivalents Julius Baer, UBS and Amundi were also hit by the blanket selling.

“When you have something as fundamental as a rewriting of the economic landscape, driven by AI, the winners will only be known in years to come,” says Martin Frandsen, a portfolio manager at Principal Asset Management.

“Right now the market is being very non-discriminatory in terms of which companies will actually be the winners and losers.”

Feverish markets

The sell-off in wealth management is just the latest tremor of a broader AI earthquake hitting stock markets.

Software companies shed $830bn (£609bn) in six days this month after Anthropic announced a new plug-in for its Claude agent that can complete tasks across data analysis, marketing, legal and sales.

In turn came new fears for the private credit sector, which has invested heavily in software companies.

On Monday, insurance stocks tumbled after OpenAI approved the first AI insurance app on ChatGPT.

On Wednesday and Thursday, property companies companies similarly saw their share prices plummet on fears bots may replace estate agents.

Even the trucking and deliver industry has been hit. Shares were sent into tailspin after Algorhythm Holdings, a former maker of karaoke systems for cars, unveiled an AI tool designed to optimise freight efficiencies. The business triggered turmoil despite having a valuation of just $6m (£4.4m).

Christian Dery, of Capital Fund Management, warns: “Firms with generic business models, low barriers to entry and high risk of automation will not survive.”

The wealth sell-off is not so much because Hazel is a seismic technological breakthrough – it’s more because markets are feverish.

“You have all the fast money hedge funds selling whoever is seen as the next AI loser straight away,” says Emmanuel Cau, the head of European equity strategy at Barclays.

Analysts think the AI tool itself is not necessarily revolutionary.

Its updated software may be able to read payslips, bank statements, meeting notes, email and customer data but it is designed to help human advisors create personalised tax strategies, not replace them.

“It is very much fear driving this rather than fundamentals,” says Cau. “The market is moving to who is the next loser, the next victim, of this AI disruption phase.

“Things are moving very fast, and it’s very hard to keep pace and a lot of investors are getting hit. It is a massive pain trade.”

AI ‘reality is dawning’

While the tool itself may not be revolutionary, investors and the industry are alarmed at the pace at which the technology is evolving.

Susannah Streeter, chief investment strategist at the Wealth Club, says: “It’s not like the warning signs haven’t been here.

“It’s just that I don’t think the wider industry really believed that they could work quite so quickly, or couldn’t really understand exactly how they would be disruptive. I think that reality is dawning right now.”

For wealth managers and advisers, the fear is AI could ultimately drive down fees, which are typically opaque and vary significantly between firms. Analysts do not think that companies will go under, but their ability to charge may be significantly degraded and that has massive implications for their share prices.

“I think it has sent a bit of a shiver through the industry, this feeling that fees may be compressed in the future, this feeling that it’s going to be a lot harder to win customers and charge substantial fees for it,” says Streeter.

Wealth managers are trying to put a brave face on it.

Speaking at UBS’s Financial Services Conference on Tuesday, Jed Finn, the head of wealth management at Morgan Stanley, said: “It is the opposite. I think AI is going to enhance the quality of advice, and it’s going to help advisors scale and be able to serve more clients more effectively with the same set of resources.”

Human element trust

City folk argue that there are limits on how far people will be willing to use ChatGPT or other AI tools to make investment decisions.

“Everyone has heard about the so-called hallucinations of large language models, that ChatGPT-type applications can sometimes give you a very incorrect answer with a lot of confidence,” says Alentorn.

“When it comes to important, potentially life-changing decisions like the deployment of capital, a catastrophic error would really have a big impact on someone’s life.”

He adds: “Clearly what’s important there is the role of the human being in creating and designing those tools and making sure that the right safeguards are in place.”

There is more to the human element than just making sure AI is fit for purpose, too.

“For all of the technological and efficiency improvements that AI has the potential to bring to the wealth industry, we don’t think it, or other automated solutions, can ever fully replace the ‘human empathy’ and ‘trust’ side of being a financial adviser,” says David McCann, an analyst at Deutsche Bank.

However, he acknowledges that “hand holding” during market downturns may be less important to “more digitally native, younger individuals” – especially as they become more confident and trusting of AI.

It is a possibility that a 25-year-old who is starting to build a portfolio today may never feel they need a human wealth manager.

“Think about all of those people who’ve already got a relationship with their chatbot. They’re asking them for relationship advice, for health advice,” says Streeter.

“It’s not a great leap to expect them to be trusting a chatbot to give them the best financial advice.”

data.title: Royal favourite Frog Bikes facing administration
data.description:
A children’s bike-maker favoured by Prince Louis is facing an uncertain future after filing an intention to appoint administrators.

Frog Bikes, founded in 2013 by husband-and-wife team Jerry and Shelley Lawson, made a court application through its law firm Shoosmiths on Friday to hire administrators, legal filings show.

Filing an intention to appoint an administrator is a legal step taken by companies to gain temporary protection from creditors. It can lead to a full-blown liquidation of a company or reorganisation of its finances.

Frog Bikes said the move was a “precautionary measure” to allow the company to keep trading while it explores “potential funding and restructuring solutions”.

Mr Lawson blamed the predicament on a “series of significant and well-documented challenges” – including higher employee taxes, Brexit, and low consumer confidence.

He added, “At the same time, tighter financial markets and reduced access to funding made it increasingly difficult for growing manufacturing businesses to secure the requisite working capital required to navigate these challenges.”

‘Continuing friction’ from Brexit

The Wales-based group has gained a cult following and won a dream endorsement when the Princess of Wales published a photo of her son riding one of the company’s products on his third birthday.

The business makes child-specific bikes after the founders said they struggled to find suitable ones for their own children.

Its lightweight aluminium bikes are made in Pontypool, Wales, where Mr and Mrs Lawson opened a 120,000-square-foot manufacturing facility.

After its success in the UK, it quickly became a market leader for affordable children’s bikes and rapidly expanded to more than 50 countries – including the US and Hong Kong.

However, Frog Bikes has recently warned that cumbersome export taxes were eating into profits.

In 2023, it lost £530,000 after blaming “continuing friction” from Brexit, which had made it harder to compete with European rivals.

However, in its most recent accounts for the year to February 2024, auditors said the group would continue as a going concern.

The report added that despite inflation falling at the time, consumers were still cautious in their spending on “big ticket purchases”, adding that there was competition from new entrants to the lightweight children’s bike market.

Bike market in turmoil

Cycling businesses have faced a difficult time as enthusiasm for the activity starts to wane.

A pandemic boom led many companies to expand rapidly, but after the interest faded, firms complained that the sudden drop in interest had left manufacturers with excess inventory.

Companies like Brompton Bicycles saw profits fall by more than 99pc in the year to March 2024. The following year, it reported pre-tax losses of £2m. At the time, the company described the bike market as being “in turmoil”.

Raleigh, another British brand, reported a loss of £30m in 2023, citing overstocked inventory following the pandemic.

Frog Bikes confirmed in a statement that the search for a new investor would continue.

Mr Lawson said: “All parties are committed to securing a positive outcome for the business, its employees and wider stakeholders.

“Frog Bikes’ priority remains supporting its employees, customers, suppliers, and retail partners while working with its advisers to determine the best possible outcome for all stakeholders.”

data.title: Labour hammers Northern Ireland with new green shipping tax
data.description:
Northern Ireland businesses are set to be hammered by a green tax which bosses fear will make them uncompetitive.

A new tax on ferries to Northern Ireland, set to be introduced this summer, will swell freight rates – weighing on the economy and driving up prices.

The levy system, known as the emissions trading scheme (ETS), stands to increase shipping costs to the province by 6pc when it comes into force in July, according industry estimates.

The UK Chamber of Shipping said that will put more pressure on an economy where supply chain costs are already higher than the rest of the UK because of arrangements imposed under Brexit’s Windsor Framework.

It said the tax will also add to the red tape foisted on business following the agreement, which placed Northern Ireland in the EU’s single market for goods after Brexit.

The ETS scheme requires shipping firms to pay for their CO2 emissions as part of the Government’s push to reach net zero by 2050.

Unlike other ferry routes, Northern Ireland will not be exempt from the charges, even though 90pc of commercial and passenger traffic goes by sea.

The Chamber said ferry firms will most likely have to pass the additional charges on.

Stena Line, which operates ferries to Belfast from Liverpool, Heysham in Lancashire and Cairnryan in Scotland, estimates that the scheme will increase its costs by £9m annually. That would average £15 for every HGV, van and car.

The Chamber said an initial 50pc deduction to match the carbon charges on voyages between Britain and Republic of Ireland will provide little relief.

That is because exporters in Northern Ireland compete mostly with firms in Britain rather than the province’s southern neighbour, to which they will be at a structural disadvantage.

UK firms have already spent millions on some of the most sustainably designed ferries, including methanol and rotor sail propulsion, leaving no financially viable way forward, it claimed.

The Chamber said: “The sector supports the UK’s climate goals, but cannot deliver meaningful emissions reduction without the necessary fuels, infrastructure, and clear guidance in place.

“Premature implementation risks higher costs for passengers and freight, with limited environmental gain. However, the Government is pressing ahead, leaving industry in an untenable position.”

The trade group urged ministers to delay introduction of the scheme while further assessments are carried out and said the revenue raised should be ring-fenced for investment in clean fuels and quayside power connections.

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said that it is for ship operators to decide how much of the expense of the ETS scheme to pass on, and that consumers should not see significant costs.

A spokesman said: “Pricing emissions is a cost-effective way to cut carbon emissions and encourage greener travel. We are working closely with industry on implementation.”

data.title: Baroness Lawrence’s hacking claims ‘wounding’, says ex-Mail editor
data.description:
Paul Dacre, the former editor of the Daily Mail, has described Baroness Lawrence’s allegations of criminality at the newspaper as “bewildering and bitterly wounding”.

Mr Dacre, 77, said the suggestion that his high-profile campaign to bring her son Stephen’s killers to justice was driven by a desire to boost sales and make a profit was “sickeningly misplaced and bleakly cynical”.

Baroness Lawrence is among seven high-profile claimants suing Associated Newspapers Limited for breach of privacy, having been alerted to her potential claim by the Duke of Sussex.

She has alleged that she was “extensively targeted” by private investigators who bugged her phone and put her under surveillance on behalf of the Mail.

Stephen Lawrence, an 18-year-old aspiring architect, was stabbed to death by a group of up to six white youths as he waited at a bus stop in Eltham, south-east London, in April 1993.

The Daily Mail, under the editorship of Mr Dacre, was at the forefront of the fight to bring his killers to justice, branding the five men – Gary Dobson, Neil Acourt, Jamie Acourt, Luke Knight, and David Norris – “murderers” in February 1997 and challenging them to sue for libel.

Mr Dacre told the court on Tuesday: “Throughout my 26-year editorship, this, of all my countless campaigns, many of which made a significant contribution to the public weal, is the campaign of which I am most proud and to which I devoted the most space.”

Mr Dacre, now the editor-in-chief of DMG Media, the holding company of Associated Newspapers, was the first witness for the defence to give evidence.

The judge had previously been told that it was considered “critically important” he went “out over the top first” to address the allegations before sending the “troops into battle”.

Mr Dacre, speaking so softly that he was barely audible, told the court that while he “clearly” wished to clear his name, he was more concerned about the reputation of the Daily Mail and its “honest and dedicated” staff, for whom he said he was “very angry and upset”.

“The grave and sometimes preposterous allegations made in these proceedings have astonished, appalled and – in the small hours of the night – reduced me to rage,” he added in his 52-page witness statement.

Challenged about his lack of acknowledgement of the impact on the claimants, he replied: “My heart bleeds for Baroness Doreen Lawrence, but these are allegations at the moment.”

Mr Dacre said he “utterly rejects” that the Mail hacked and tapped phones, adding that he had “never” placed a story in the Daily Mail as a result of phone hacking and knew of no cases of phone hacking at the Mail.

Such “blatant illegality” would not have been countenanced at any level, and it would have been “insanity” to risk damaging his reputation by allowing such activity to take place, he added.

David Sherborne, the barrister acting for all seven claimants, has repeatedly suggested that Mr Dacre gave false information to the 2011-12 Leveson Inquiry about his newspaper’s use of private investigators and in his insistence that there was “no phone hacking on the Daily Mail”.

Prince Harry told the court he could remember Mr Dacre “clearly stating that phone hacking had not taken place at Associated” and was “so convinced that I didn’t even think twice about it”.

Mr Dacre “emphatically” denied that he had lied to the inquiry about his ban on using private investigators, phone hacking, computer hacking and paying police officers.

He explained to the court that the emergence of private investigators at the turn of the millennium presented a “totally new landscape for journalism” and that most journalists had an “extremely hazy understanding” of the ramifications of the new digital technology and data protection laws.

However, he said he had no recollection of being told of any particular instances or allegations of ”blagging” by journalists or “inquiry agents” during his time as editor and would have been “pretty furious” if significant private information had been blagged that was not in the public interest.

After almost two hours of cross-examination, Mr Justice Nicklin warned Mr Sherborne that the bulk of his questions to Mr Dacre had not addressed the specific claims and said he was “struggling” to understand their relevance.

He warned that the case was not a public inquiry and gave him until 3pm on Wednesday to complete his cross-examination.

Associated is a subsidiary of Daily Mail and General Trust, Lord Rothermere’s holding company, which has agreed to acquire The Telegraph for £500m. The proposed takeover is expected to face regulatory scrutiny in the coming weeks.

The trial continues.

data.title: How mobile phone providers are getting away with 22pc price rises
data.description:
Have you been hit by a mid-contract price increase on your phone bill? Get in touch with us at money@telegraph.co.uk*.

Mobile phone network providers have been accused of “milking” customers with mid-contract price rises as high as 22pc.

Last year regulator Ofcom banned phone providers from including inflation-linked or percentage-based price rises in new contracts over concerns that the practice left customers with uncertainty about the level of future increases.

Customer contracts must now state at the point of sale what any future price rises will be in pounds and pence.

But consumer experts have warned that the new rules have resulted in even bigger increases for customers compared to when bills typically rose by inflation plus 3.9pc.

One O2 customer told The Telegraph that his phone bill rose mid-contract by 22pc last April, after the provider increased prices by £1.80 across the board. This far outstripped the 7.3pc increase he would have seen under the old system.

Consumer group Which? has warned that customers on cheaper tariffs could be disproportionately impacted by fixed uplifts, as they would likely see larger percentage increases to their bill. For example, a £2.50 rise for a £10 tariff amounts to a 25pc uplift, whereas the same £2.50 increase on a £30 tariff equates to a rise of just 8pc.

Martin Lewis, the Money Saving Expert, previously said that Ofcom’s new rules were “flaccid” and that they “effectively embed” above-inflation mid-contract price rises across the industry.

He added: “I don’t understand why we don’t grasp what is by far the simplest and most effective solution: ban firms from increasing prices above inflation during a fixed-term contract.”

Britain’s biggest mobile phone providers – Vodafone and Three (which recently merged), Sky, Virgin Media, O2 and EE (which is a part of BT) – signed a new consumer charter on Wednesday that the Government has said will prevent surprise phone bill increases. However, the policy change still won’t ban mid-term above-inflation increases.

The agreement was in part drawn up in response to an announcement by O2 last year that it would increase bills by £2.50 from April 2026, more than the £1.80 customers had signed up to.

Ofcom said at the time that the uplift went against the spirit of its new rules, which the provider was able to circumvent by allowing customers to leave mid-contract without a penalty within 30 days.

Firms such as Sky include “variable pricing” structures in tariffs which are allowed because the customer has a right to leave within 30 days of being notified.

New consumer rights have ‘backfired’

Business consultant Andy Reed was among those stung by a mid-contract price rise last April.

The 64-year-old, an O2 customer, initially signed up to a one-year SIM-only tariff at £7.99 a month in July 2024. Nine months later, his bill was increased to £9.79, a 22pc rise.

He said: “The fact that my bill has gone up by 22pc in less than a year is frankly outrageous.

“Ofcom’s new rules are simpler but it means operators can exploit it to make more money. People know what their new bill is going to be, but when you look at it in percentage terms, it’s the operators milking their lowest-paying customers. It’s backfired.”

An O2 spokesman said: “An annual rise of £2.50 a month – around 8p a day – continues to represent excellent value for services that customers are using more than ever before.

“We’ve again frozen prices on handset repayment plans and are investing £700m into our mobile network this year to ensure we meet growing demand and give our customers the fast and reliable connectivity they rely on.”

‘Customers ripped off’

All four of Britain’s largest mobile phone network providers are expected to raise prices for most customers under contract by a fixed amount this spring.

The exact rise can vary depending on when you signed your contract, which provider you use and how much data you pay for. Vodafone, Three and EE increase prices in line with what customers agreed to when they signed their contract.

Dr Luke Evans, a Conservative MP, said: “It’s more than discouraging to hear, after Ofcom introduced rules to address this, that some increases are at a higher percentage than when increases were set to inflation plus 3.9pc. It’s no wonder that customers, who are tied into a contract, feel they are being completely ripped off.”

The Telegraph has seen the bill of one customer who is under contract with Three which will rise from £7 to £8.25 in April, representing a 17pc increase. One EE customer’s bill is poised to increase by 9pc to £28.80 in March.

Ernest Doku, of comparison website Uswitch, said: “The best defence is to keep track of your contract end date as switching the moment you’re free allows you to reset your bill and avoid these increases compounding on top of expensive out-of-contract rates.”

Citizens Advice has now called for an outright ban on mid-contract price rises.

The charity’s Anne Pardoe said: “When inflation was at an all-time high, Ofcom was right to act to protect consumers from unpredictable, inflation-linked telecoms price hikes mid-contract. But what started as a well-intentioned reform has left many people facing bigger price rises.

“By focusing on pricing transparency from telecom providers, the regulator has inadvertently made the affordability problem worse. Greater clarity shouldn’t come at greater cost.”

An Ofcom spokesman said: “These commitments build on the significant protections already in place in our competitive telecoms markets – from simpler switching and social tariffs to warning customers when contracts are ending and protecting people from volatile and unpredictable inflation.

“At the same time, broadband and mobile companies are investing heavily in upgrading and expanding their networks, delivering greater choice and better quality while average prices have been falling in real terms.

A VodafoneThree spokesman said: “To give customers greater certainty and transparency, in line with Ofcom’s guidelines, we moved to a pounds-and-pence approach where any mid-contract price rise is clearly set out upfront at the point of sale, so customers know exactly what they will pay across their contract term, and that fixed amount does not change during the contract.”

An EE spokesman said: “We remain committed to Ofcom’s requirement to display upfront pounds-and-pence charges across the full length of a customer’s contract. We will be contacting every customer to explain what price change means for them and the options available. We are not adjusting the annual price change for customers within the minimum term of their contract.”

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: The Cornish mine that could help Trump in his new arms race with China
data.description:
Britain’s last operational tin mine, South Crofty, has been sitting dormant in Cornwall for nearly 30 years. But now the historic site may be revived thanks to an unlikely benefactor: Donald Trump.

The US president’s administration last week said it was willing to loan up to $225m (£165m) to developer Cornish Metals to support the mine’s reopening – so long as it agrees to send some of the output across the Atlantic.

Just two days earlier, and more than 4,600 miles away, a Washington-backed investment fund had announced a deal to acquire a 40pc share in a copper and cobalt mine run by Glencore in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

And in Western Australia, the US government has emerged as one of the backers of a scheme to build a new refinery that will produce gallium, a rare metal.

The investments follow a spate of dealmaking by the Trump administration at home in the past year – which has seen it take stakes in a magnet producer, a lithium mine and rare earth refineries – among other things.

According to experts, they are all examples of the new, more muscular approach Washington is taking to securing critical minerals abroad as it seeks to break China’s stranglehold over global supplies.

Tin, copper, cobalt and gallium are all commonly used in electronics, while lithium – known as “white gold” – is the main ingredient in batteries that power the modern world.

Rare earth magnets, meanwhile, are found in the spinning parts of wind turbines, electric motors and even fighter plane wing flaps.

Without them, and a plethora of other metals, factory production lines across America would grind to a halt.

That briefly became a real prospect last year, when the US was locked in a trade standoff with China.

“One of the challenges we’ve had in the last 20 years in the United States is our mineral security efforts have largely been contained to handshake diplomacy,” says Gracelin Baskaran, a critical minerals expert at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS).

“But handshake diplomacy doesn’t bring projects online. And it doesn’t yield minerals that can meet critical security interests.

“It’s the start, but what is the support that we need to get projects into production? It’s financing, it’s trade agreements, it’s off-take agreements [where a customer agrees to buy raw material before it is produced]. That is how we increase access to minerals.

“We’re going from paper agreements to deploying capital.”

US trailing China

This is the thinking behind the dizzying series of deals announced by American officials in recent months.

Efforts to start directly nurturing alternative supplies began under Joe Biden but have accelerated under Trump, with investments made through several arms of the state – including the department of war, the department of energy, the Export-Import Bank of the United States (Exim) and the US International Development Finance Corporation (DFC).

The DFC was the investor that supported Glencore’s copper and cobalt operations, while Exim has extended the offer of loans and financing to the tin project being developed by Cornish Metals.

Yet the Trump administration is still playing catch-up.

Chinese companies have been steadily building their control over key mining and refining operations for decades, giving Beijing the ability to turn off supplies to the rest of the world at will.

Take just one example: copper, the world’s most important industrial metal, used in electrical wiring, circuit boards and plumbing. Analysts predict the world is headed for a supply crunch in the coming years – and China is far better placed to weather it.

The Middle Kingdom’s companies control around 16pc of global copper production compared to 6pc owned by American firms, according to data from Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, and there have been calls in China to boost domestic stockpiles.

“China has been doing this for a while, so this isn’t new for them,” says Albert Mackenzie, an analyst at Benchmark.

“The US may have looked at how they have built up their copper assets overseas, and is now trying to replicate that or not get left behind.

“There’s a big desire for more copper at the moment – you can point at AI, or to the energy transition, but actually it’s in basically everything we use because it’s such a good conductor.”

Minerals monopoly

While China has a grip on supply, its dominance of critical minerals is really about processing, with its share standing at over 50pc for a whole host of metals and exceeding 90pc for certain products.

This means that even though such metals may be mined in the US, Australia or elsewhere, they are still sent to China for refining.

Some 91pc of rare earth magnet metal refining and 94pc of global magnet production is concentrated in China, according to the International Energy Agency.

These are the magnets used in critical parts of the US military’s F-35 stealth fighter, as well as Tomahawk cruise missiles, navy destroyers and submarines, the Stinger portable missile and the M1 Abrams tank.

This leverage was brought into painful focus for Mr Trump last year, when he was forced to walk back his threats of trade tariffs after Beijing imposed export restrictions on heavy rare earths.

Ahead of his visit to China in April, the US president has now approved the creation of a $12bn critical mineral reserve, known as Project Vault, that will seek to guard US manufacturers against similar threats in future.

The initiative has been backed by captains of industry including the bosses of GE Vernova and Boeing.

“We don’t want to ever go through what we went through a year ago,” Mr Trump told reporters last week.

“Just as we have long had a strategic petroleum reserve and a stockpile of critical minerals for national defence, we’re now creating this reserve for American industry, so we don’t have any problems.”

Security and stability

The US administration has also been seeking to rally allies around an international network for developing critical mineral supplies outside of China.

Last week, vice-president JD Vance and Marco Rubio, the secretary of state, hosted a critical minerals summit in Washington aimed at drumming up support for the initiative.

In a speech, Mr Rubio pointed out that the Chinese grip over supplies posed the same danger to all of America’s allies and urged them to work together.

“No country in the world has every critical mineral that’s valuable,” he said. “So it will rely on international cooperation.

“Right now, you have 90-something per cent of some of these elements controlled by one nation, one place. And that’s just not healthy for the global economy and it’s certainly not healthy for security and stability.”

Along with the deals that have been announced, the White House has also vowed to back minerals producers with grants, loans and even “price floors” that will ensure they get paid a minimum amount for their minerals.

This is seen as vital because China’s producers receive large amounts of state support and can otherwise undercut competition from private Western firms.

Dominic Raab, the former Conservative deputy prime minister who now works with minerals investor Appian Capital Advisory, was in the room and says this change in mindset is key.

“There’s a recognition that it’s not a free market, it’s a broken market,” he says. “So they’re going to have to engage at a state level, but they also want to unlock private sector investment, and that’s important for de-risking those investments and unlocking more capital.”

Sophia Gaston, a geopolitics researcher at King’s College London, says the showdown with Beijing last year left Washington officials chastened by America’s “profound vulnerabilities on these supply chains”.

“China called the Trump administration’s bluff, and that forced America back to the negotiation table, and I think they felt extremely exposed by that, and wanted to move quickly to try and prevent that kind of situation happening again,” she says.

“There’s been a very intense flurry of activity, because Donald Trump is pencilled in for a visit to Beijing in April. So I think he wants to feel he’s going into that conversation with the maximum leverage.

“Until that critical minerals and rare earths diversification strategy starts to come into view, that’s going to be difficult – because there will always be a feeling that China can turn off the tap.”
