data.title: Time to remortgage? How to get the best deal – even if it’s more expensive
data.description:
Around 1.9 million British households will come to the end of fixed-rate mortgage deals this year, according to UK Finance – which will be welcome news for some and rather worrying for others.

According to information obtained from the Financial Conduct Authority by Compare The Market, 971,105 five-year fixed-rate mortgages were taken out in 2021 at historically low rates averaging around 0.91pc. The majority of these households will be looking to remortgage again this year. With the cheapest fixes now between 3.5pc and 4pc, their repayments may increase accordingly.

At the other end of the spectrum are those borrowers coming off expensive two-year fixed rates – who will likely be glad to see the back of them. These households took out mortgages in 2024 when average two-year rates were at around 5.7pc. These borrowers have already experienced a spike in repayments and are likely to see their monthly repayments reduce significantly when they remortgage this year.

Whether your mortgage repayments are predicted to go up or get cheaper, Telegraph Money explains how to make the best financial decision with your next mortgage move.

  • My mortgage costs are going up
  • My mortgage costs are going down

My cheap five-year fix is ending

How much will my repayments go up?

This will depend on a number of factors, including your current mortgage rate and term, whether it’s interest-only or repayment, the size of a loan, the proportion you’re borrowing (the loan-to-value ratio) and your financial circumstances. But to get a sense of what to expect, John Everest of Everest Mortgage Services Ltd has put together an example using some average figures.

Let’s say Joe bought a property for £270,000 with a £20,000 deposit, borrowing £250,000 on a 35-year mortgage term. He has a repayment mortgage and is about to finish a cheap 1.29pc five-year fixed deal, where he’d been paying £740 a month.

From 2026, when he comes to remortgage, his outstanding balance would have been reduced to £226,000, but with a new deal at 3.79pc his monthly repayments will still increase to £1,050.

“The new rate is likely to be more expensive ... however, fortunately, many of the fixed rates available today are the cheapest we have seen for about three years,” said Adrian Anderson, of broker Anderson Harris.

What should I do before my deal ends?

While the numbers can look terrifying, there are ways you can improve your financial position before your current deal comes to an end.

  • Six months beforehand – speak to a mortgage broker: Starting the process early will help you understand how much your payments are likely to increase, giving you time to prepare. “Around six months before your deal ends, you can usually secure a new rate with an adviser that offers a ‘price match promise’, meaning if rates improve before completion, the deal can be re-priced and resubmitted at the lower rate,” said Sarah Tucker, chief executive and founder of The Mortgage Mum.
  • Consider overpayments: Most fixed-rate deals come with the option to overpay 10pc of the loan value without incurring an early repayment charge. By taking advantage of the lower rate of your existing mortgage to overpay, you can not only cut the term of your overall mortgage, but if it gets you in a lower borrowing threshold, you could secure a cheaper deal.
  • Build up your savings: If you are on a very low mortgage rate, for example, 0.9pc, it might make more fiscal sense to build up your savings if you’ll receive a higher rate of interest on them than you will save by overpaying your mortgage. “Putting that money into a high-interest savings account or Isa helps households adjust to the higher monthly cost in advance, and it also creates a buffer that can be used for overpayments once the new mortgage starts, reducing the loan balance and long-term interest,” said Ms Tucker.

How to choose the best deal

With hundreds of deals to choose from – none of which are likely to be as “good” as the one you’ve just been on – it can be hard to tell which is the most suitable option. Assuming you’ve already enlisted the help of a mortgage broker, as suggested in the step above, here are some other pointers to secure the best deal for you.

  • Ask your lender about product transfer deals: Many lenders like to retain existing customers, so explore what options they are offering early and compare them to competitors to secure the best possible deal you can.
  • Don’t be wowed by the headline rate: Lenders might advertise low interest rates, but these can mask the effect of high arrangement or product fees, which can be four-figure sums. Also check to see whether free valuations are included. Depending on the size of your mortgage, it might work out better value to select a higher interest rate with no product fees.
  • Reassess your borrowing: Since you last took out a mortgage, at the same time as you’ve been paying down your debt, your home will almost certainly have increased in value, thereby increasing the equity you hold. The more equity you have in your home, the more favourable mortgage products you have access to – so make sure your lender makes an accurate assessment of how much your home is worth, particularly if you’ve made improvements with extensions or building work in the last five years.
  • Avoid the SVR: If you haven’t switched to a new mortgage deal by the time your fixed rate comes to an end, you’ll need to act quickly to avoid automatically being placed on your current lender’s Standard Variable Rate (SVR). This is likely to be one of the most expensive rates out there, usually around 7pc, and means your payments will jump up significantly.

“If a fixed rate has already ended, the priority is acting quickly rather than fixating on a specific product,” said Mr Tucker. “Borrowers can switch to a temporary tracker rate with no Early Repayment Charge with their current lender for breathing space while a full market review is carried out.” This is preferential to the SVR, which, even over just a few weeks, could end up costing thousands of pounds.

Five-year fix, two-year fix or tracker – how to choose

The decision of what type of mortgage you opt for next is a personal one, but there are certain factors you should consider. Around 85pc of mortgages in the UK are currently fixed policies – most commonly two- or five-year fixes, but a tracker mortgage might be a better option for some. The rates for these deals are tied to movements in the Bank Rate, so can increase or decrease at any time.

Choose a two-year fix if:

  • You want more flexibility; perhaps you’re planning to move after two years, or intend to repay a chunk of your mortgage with a matured investment or a pension lump sum, and want to avoid any early repayment charges.
  • You want a cheaper rate than five-year fixes offer.

Choose a five-year fix if:

  • You want longer-term certainty and are happy to be tied into a mortgage for five years.
  • It’s unlikely that your financial situation will change significantly over the term of the deal.
  • You aren’t planning on moving house.

Choose a tracker if:

  • You want to be able to leave your mortgage at any time without paying a large early repayment charge.
  • You are happy with a ‘riskier’ loan that could increase if the Bank Rate rises and can afford repayments if the rates were to increase.
  • You expect the Bank Rate to go down and want to take advantage of this.

I’m coming off an expensive two-year fix

How much will my repayments go down?

Using more figures from Mr Everest, let’s take another borrower, Tom, who purchased a property for £270,000 on a 35-year term, with a repayment mortgage of £250,000. His two-year deal charged 6pc interest and cost him £1,426 a month. When he comes to remortgage, he secures a new deal at 4pc, which means his repayments reduce by £309 a month to £1,117.

How to make the most of lower mortgage payments

In terms of getting a new mortgage, much of the advice above will also apply to this group, but being in the fortunate position of having some money freed up by a cheaper deal means you have additional options of how to make the most of it.

While it might be tempting to use the extra money for life’s little luxuries, allowing lifestyle creep is not a great use of your cash – but these options could put you on stronger financial footing in the future:

  • Use extra cash for overpayments: If you are accustomed to higher monthly payments, you could continue repaying them after you’ve switched to a cheaper deal. “If your lifestyle was adjusted around the more expensive mortgage rates, now could be a good time to try and reduce the mortgage on a monthly basis with regular overpayments,” said Mr Anderson. “[This] can reduce the amount of mortgage interest you pay on the term of your mortgage and can shorten the mortgage term.”
  • Opt for other investments: You might want to use the money for another purpose, in which case you could invest this extra cash rather than overpay. “For some households, especially those without existing investments, directing surplus money into a long-term strategy such as a stocks and shares Isa can be equally effective,” said Ms Tucker.
  • Create a slush fund or emergency savings: You could also put the extra monthly cash towards building up savings. This is particularly important if you’ve exhausted them to stay on top of the higher mortgage repayments over the last two years.

data.title: British boss of Wall Street bank to get £30m after pay rise
data.description:
The British-born chief executive of US bank Citi has seen her pay rise to $42m (£31m), elevating her into the ranks of the best-paid leaders on Wall Street.

Jane Fraser, who hails from Scotland, saw her total pay climb 22pc in 2025, Citi said on Thursday night.

Her total $42m package ranks her compensation just below that of Jamie Dimon, the JP Morgan boss who earned $43m, and David Solomon, the Goldman Sachs boss who was awarded $47m.

The pay rise comes after Ms Fraser was elected chairman of the Wall Street bank alongside her post as chief executive last year.

Her deal also makes her among the best-paid British business executives globally and one of the top earning businesswomen in the world.

Ms Fraser was born in St Andrews, Fife, and has held the post of chief executive of Citi since 2021.

Educated at Cambridge and Harvard, the 58-year-old joined the bank in 2004 after a stint with McKinsey before rising through the ranks to its most senior position.

The Scottish banker took over as chief executive of Citi from Michael Corbat, who had previously said he did not expect to be replaced by a woman. She remains the first and only female chief executive among the biggest banks on Wall Street.

“One of the pieces of advice I was given when I became CEO is [to] ‘have big ears and thick skin’,” Ms Fraser told broadcaster Channel News Asia in 2023. “I strongly believe in excellence and empathy, and empathy to me is the big ears.”

Ms Fraser has overseen job cuts and axed layers of management at Citi, helping to improve performance.

Pay on Wall Street has jumped over the past year after a surge in deal-making and recovery in US stock market listings helped to boost revenues. The prospect of rate cuts by the Federal Reserve and optimism over the artificial intelligence boom have also helped propel bank stocks higher. Citi’s shares surged more than 65pc last year.

At Goldman Sachs, Mr Solomon’s climbed 21pc to $47m last year. Ted Pick, the boss of Morgan Stanley, saw his pay package jump 32pc to $45m after the bank’s shares climbed nearly 45pc over the year.

Ms Fraser is one of only a handful of women to have secured roles at the very top of the world’s biggest banks.

In the UK, Alison Rose was the chief executive of NatWest until 2023. Marianne Lake, a British-American senior executive at JP Morgan, is seen as a leading contender to replace Mr Dimon, its current chief executive.

data.title: Killer motorists should be treated like the murderers they are
data.description:
April 25 2007 is etched forever in my mind. It was a beautiful day, and summer was just around the corner. My twin sister had given birth to a baby boy, and my 65-year-old mother, Elizabeth, had come to stay with me in Mortlake, south London. We planned to go to the Chelsea and Westminster Hospital to visit the new baby.

My husband walked my two eldest children, Barnaby and Sapphire – who were six and four at the time – to school and then hopped on a train to work. My mother, my two-year-old daughter, Pollyanna, and I set off to catch the bus to the hospital.

My mother, who lived in rural Shropshire, loved London buses and much preferred them to the train.

We walked, with Pollyanna in her pram, the short distance from our house to the local bus depot. As we approached, Pollyanna climbed out of her buggy and held my mother’s hand.

This was when my life changed irreversibly. I turned around, and all I could see was a monstrous, red Metroline bus approaching at a ferocious speed. Instead of turning into the depot, it drove on to the pavement, aiming straight for us. It happened so fast – almost like a bomb going off.

My legs were trapped under the bus, and I was like the jam in the middle of a horrid sandwich – bus, me, wall. Blood was everywhere. I slipped in and out of consciousness. Out of one eye, I saw my darling, precious, beautiful mother slipping away. She did not survive.

Out of my other eye, I saw my little daughter about 20 ft away. She had been flung through the air, and her right leg was hanging off. I was trapped and could not help her. I shouted for my mother so loudly. I wanted her to answer me, but she did not. She could not.

The crash quickly flooded the news as my husband – The Telegraph’s Christopher Hope – received a call at work to tell him what had happened.

As he made the terrible journey from his office in Victoria to Mortlake, he did not even know if we were alive or not. Four surgeons spent seven hours trying to save Pollyanna’s leg, but they could not, and she had it amputated.

I suffered severe degloving in my leg (where skin and soft tissue is torn from the muscle or bone underneath), among other injuries. However, knowing that I would never see my mother again was the most unbearable wound.

The bus driver who hit us was found guilty of one count of death by dangerous driving and two counts of grievous bodily harm. He was sentenced to four years in prison, which is simply not long enough. Often, only aggravating factors like being under the influence of drink or drugs result in a longer sentence, but it is rare that criminal drivers get more than 12 years behind bars.

On average, four people die every day on UK roads, and many dozens more suffer life-changing injuries. It is no wonder that victims of road crime – like me – feel utterly let down by the UK’s criminal justice system.

Metroline would not speak to me when I rang up their head office. I felt upset, hopeless and angry. So, in February 2014, I set out on a mission – for road crime to be treated as real crime, and for victims and their families to be treated with kindness and compassion in navigating the criminal justice system.

I met Boris Johnson, who was the mayor of London at the time. He said he was very sorry for what had happened to my family. He asked me whether Transport for London (TfL) had apologised, and I said no.

He told me that must change. A few weeks later, my husband and I met with TfL and Metroline. They said they were very sorry. I said that “sorry” must hold meaning, and things began to change from then on.

The Sarah Hope Line was launched in February 2016. It is an incident line that offers confidential support to people involved in, or affected by, life-changing incidents on the TfL network. We have provided to more than 500 families what TfL did not give to us: trauma counselling, assistance with funeral costs and arrangements, and short-term accommodation for family members. Next Monday will mark our 10-year anniversary.

While the line supports families of the bereaved, we still have not yet changed the attitude within government that road crashes are “just one of those things”. This Government should spend much more on policing our roads, so that criminal drivers can be caught. We need to issue immediate fines and driving bans so motorists are deterred from driving dangerously.

Road crime and the damage it causes cost the taxpayer billions of pounds every year – it strains the NHS, criminal justice system and our emergency services. Fatalities, alone, cost the NHS billions every year. That money should be spent on policing our roads and preventing such devastation.

Perpetrators think they can get away with causing death by dangerous driving because sentences do not reflect the severity of the crime. Change must start now. We must no longer be the forgotten victims.

The Sarah Hope Line is on 0343 222 5678, Monday to Friday, 8am-6pm. For more details, go to https://tfl.gov.uk/travel-information/safety/incident-support-service

data.title: NHS contractor Palantir will suffer $200bn wipeout, says Big Short investor
data.description:
The US investor made famous by the film The Big Short is predicting that NHS contractor Palantir will have $200bn (£147bn) knocked off its value in the coming years.

Michael Burry, who correctly predicted the housing market crash before the 2008 financial crisis, said Palantir’s success in recent years was built on “bluster” fuelled by the AI bubble.

The company, founded by Donald Trump’s ally Peter Thiel, had overplayed its AI credentials, according to Mr Burry.

He claimed that Palantir was a consultancy rather than an AI business, stressing that it could be “easily replaced”.

While it is best known for its work with intelligence and military agencies in the US, Palantir was also handed the biggest IT contract in NHS history in 2023 to bring together medical information from across the health service.

Mr Burry, who was played by Christian Bale in the 2015 Hollywood movie, said everything the company does “already exists” – claiming it had taken advantage of an “ignorance window” on how AI works.

He said: “After a few years, it is likely many clients will come off their FOMO and realise they can do it themselves cheaper.”

Palantir’s work in the UK also saw it build the Covid dashboard for the Government – which presented daily data on vaccines, deaths and hospitalisations during the pandemic.

Under its latest contract with the NHS, Palantir was tasked with joining up existing NHS data in a bid to speed up diagnosis and reduce waiting times and hospital stays.

However, official figures this week showed that A&E trolley waits have risen to their worst on record – with more NHS patients than ever facing 12-hour delays last month.

‘Emperor Palantir has no clothes’

In a post on his Substack on Thursday, Mr Burry said Palantir was little more than a consultancy which had gained attention after “management flooded its earnings calls with mentions of AI”.

He said its chief executive, Alex Karp, had initially been “blindsided by ChatGPT” and other large language models, only then to decide that he could “spin this as Palantir is AI”.

Mr Burry said: “Like Trump, Karp figures bluster has gotten him pretty far, and so will continue in that mode.”

Palantir has previously hit back against Mr Burry’s criticism. In late 2025, Mr Karp branded the investor “bats--t crazy” for predicting such sharp falls in its stock.

Mr Burry, who appeared to close down his hedge fund Scion Asset Management earlier this year, predicted Palantir would ultimately be worth less than $100bn.

That is less than a third of its current market valuation of $307bn, down from a peak of $491bn in November.

He calculated that a fair value for the shares would be around $46, which would mark a 64pc drop from its $129 closing price on Thursday.

Mr Bury said: “Palantir’s customers using its software for the first 20 years did not value it highly enough to pay rates that would make Palantir anywhere close to profitable.

“All those customers were subsidised by nearly $4bn from investors. $4bn that involved mostly the cost of installing and supporting the software rather than actual [Research and Development] developing the software.

“Now, with AI on the scene, everything is supposed to have changed.”

He added that “well-heeled competitors” in the software sector “are studying the landscape” after being bruised in a recent sell-off over concerns that AI will disrupt their business models.

Mr Burry said: “They may pounce before or after savvy customers realise Emperor Palantir has no clothes.”

Palantir declined to comment.

The Department for Health was contacted for comment.

data.title: BrewDog seeks rescuer as losses mount
data.description:
BrewDog has put itself up for sale and is seeking a rescuer as it grapples with rising taxes and years of heavy losses.

The Scottish brewer, which is one of the country’s best-known craft beer brands, has appointed restructuring experts AlixPartners to sound out possible bidders. A quick-fire deadline for indicative offers is understood to have been set.

BrewDog said it was reviewing its options in light of the “challenging economic climate”. A source close to the company said rising taxes on both alcohol and pubs had been a “massive headwind for some time”.

While sales of its beer to pubs, restaurants and supermarkets make up the majority of BrewDog’s business, the company also has dozens of bars in the UK.

Alcohol duty rose this month and pubs and bars face a steep increase in business rates under plans set out by Rachel Reeves last month. Both impact BrewDog.

The Telegraph has been campaigning to lessen the tax burden on the pub sector amid warnings of widespread closures without more support.

While the recent tax changes have put pressure on the craft brewer, BrewDog has been loss-making for years. In 2024-25, the company lost £37m on a turnover of £357m.

Sales growth has also slowed markedly amid a cost of living squeeze and as younger generations drink less, or abstain from alcohol altogether.

Many of those who are drinking are turning away from the strong flavours of craft beer, with Guinness and lager Madri performing particularly well recently. Last year The Telegraph reported that BrewDog’s beers had been axed by almost 2,000 pubs across Britain.

Brewers must also contend with high energy prices. Record numbers shut down last year and Yorkshire-based Black Sheep Brewery narrowly avoided collapse last month after managing to strike a rescue deal.

Possible suitors for BrewDog are likely to include Heineken, Asahi and ABInBev, the brewing giant behind Budweiser and Stella Artois. Carlsberg may also examine the business.

James Watt, BrewDog’s controversial co-founder, is also considering an offer for the company according to industry press. Mr Watt, who owns a 21pc stake in the company, ran BrewDog until stepping down in 2024 in the wake of allegations he created a “culture of fear”. He denied the claims.

AlixPartners’ appointment, which was first reported by Sky News, may pave the way for a break-up, with the brewing operations in Ellon, Scotland, the US, Australia and Germany hived off from the company’s 72 bars worldwide.

The decision to kick-start a sale process this week comes as TSG Consumer Partners (TSG), BrewDog’s private equity backers, seek an exit from an investment held for almost 10 years. TSG has representation on the BrewDog board, which decided to appoint the restructuring experts.

The US private equity group, based in San Francisco, invested £213m in BrewDog in 2017 in an unorthodox deal which turned the brewer’s founders into multimillionaires. The deal, which handed TSG a 21pc stake, valued the craft beer company at £895m.

Unusually, the private equity company insisted on a clause guaranteeing an 18pc compounding rate of return on its investment. 
The arrangement has effectively saddled the business with a debt to TSG of more than £800m, which may act as a deterrent for any would-be buyers.

Any sale is likely to see the thousands of small-time investors who backed BrewDog through its “Equity for Punks” crowdfunding get little or nothing for their investment.

Between 2009 and 2021, BrewDog raised £73m from 220,000 individual shareholders, with the average investor putting in £400. The most recent crowdfunding round valued the brewer at £2bn, but any sale is expected to be well below that price.

Over the past year, BrewDog has closed more than a dozen bars, cut jobs and sold a Scottish forest meant to bolster its net zero credentials as it scrambled to improve its finances.

Its downturn is in sharp contrast to its wild ascendancy, in which it won the support of a legion of millennial drinkers with its Punk IPA and rode the wave of demand for craft beer.

Mr Watt and business partner Martin Dickie founded BrewDog in Fraserburgh, Scotland, in 2007 and grew it into Britain’s biggest independent craft brewery, buoyed by marketing stunts that included driving a tank through central London and bottling a 55pc beer in embalmed stoat and squirrel carcasses.

A BrewDog spokesman said: “As with many businesses operating in a challenging economic climate and facing sustained macro headwinds, we regularly review our options with a focus on the long-term strength and sustainability of the company.

“This is a deliberate and disciplined step with a focus on strengthening the long-term future of the BrewDog brand and its operations.”

BrewDog said it remains “the number one independent brewer in the UK” and is confident its reputation will attract “substantial interest”.

“Our breweries, bars, and venues continue to operate as normal. We will not comment on any further speculation,” a spokesman said.

data.title: ‘Once it is gone, it’s gone forever’: Labour’s plan to pave over Britain’s countryside
data.description:
Our green and pleasant land is facing an attack on all fronts as Labour gets behind its “build, baby, build” mantra and overhauls planning regulations to increase rural housebuilding.

The green belt is shrinking, so-called “Nimbys” are being demonised and councillors are adopting a yes-first approach to planning applications.

It’s a worrying time for those seeking to safeguard the countryside. A recent poll of more than 2,000 people, commissioned by the Campaign to Protect Rural England (CPRE), found that Sir Keir Starmer recorded a net trust rating of -40pc when it comes to protecting Britain’s nature and countryside, the lowest among all party leaders.

Just 2pc of respondents said they want existing planning protections to be loosened, yet it is with this extreme minority that Labour has sided.

Those who speak out against a housing proposal are perceived as the enemy: the Prime Minister has described new-build naysayers as “blockers” and accused them of “suffocating the aspirations of working families and obscuring the future of our country”. Ministers have called for the pleas of “Nimbys” to have less of a foothold on the planning process.

Britain’s green belt is at risk from the Government, and it seems little can be done about it. Telegraph Money explores the ways Labour is preparing to concrete over the countryside.

1. Mandatory housing targets and 1.5 million new homes

One of the first reforms Labour made when elected was to reintroduce mandatory housebuilding targets for councils, which the Tories had previously relaxed. Local authorities must now meet strict targets, some of which have more than doubled.

This has alarmed villagers in bucolic settings. Residents in Redbourn, Hertfordshire, fear they will become “the sacrificial lambs of new planning rules” because of proposals to treble the size of their village, while those in the rolling Hampshire hills near Alton say the plans will “destroy” the nearby countryside.

The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) has continuously stated that revised housing targets are “in line with the needs of local areas, so more homes will be built in the right places alongside necessary infrastructure”.

2. Less concentration in London

While villages are being built up, London is being let off. In fact, it was the only region whose annual housebuilding target was reduced, with Angela Rayner, the former housing secretary, cutting it from 98,222 to 80,693.

The downgraded figures, however, are still far out of reach for the Labour-led capital where housebuilding starts have fallen more than 80pc over the past decade. Just 4,500 homes are likely to be finished in the capital in both 2027 and 2028, according to the consultancy firm Molior.

The slump has angered countryside campaigners, who believe they are being punished with higher housing targets as a result of London’s failure.

Damian Green, a former Cabinet minister who is now a trustee of Kent’s CPRE branch, told The Telegraph: “The demand for housing is at its most acute in London, which is precisely where new building has collapsed.

“The Government’s policies are a threat to the countryside because they do not insist on using the vast potential of brownfield land in our big cities first.”

An MHCLG spokesman said: “We inherited the worst housing crisis in living memory which is why we will build 1.5 million homes to restore the dream of home ownership.

“But this will not come at the expense of the environment. We’re clear that brownfield land should be the first option for development, and our changes to green belt policy do not weaken protections for the environment.”

3. A yes-first approach

Councils could be stripped of their power to block planning permission for projects of 150 homes or more, as part of proposals to revise national planning policy.

Should they wish to refuse permission on large developments, they will have to refer to the Government for a decision. Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, will have the power to call in the projects, appoint a planning inspector to examine each scheme and make a recommendation on whether to go ahead.

Conservative MP Mark Francois told the Commons in December: “The application could even be given to a planning inspector to deal with behind closed doors by written representations, so that local people and their councillors would not have a chance to say anything at all. That is completely undemocratic.”

In addition, there are new powers for ministers to force through “nationally significant” infrastructure projects, such as reservoirs, solar-panel parks, wind farms, pylons and electricity substations.

The Government’s proposals to water down the voice of communities are in stark contrast to language previously used by senior Labour ministers, including the Prime Minister himself. Speaking in 2020, he said: “We would give local government a much bigger say over investment and services – not through plans devised by someone in an office on Whitehall.”

4. Diluting the green belt

Around 12.5pc of UK land is classified as green belt, which creates a buffer protecting 15 large cities from urban sprawl.

But government officials have argued that swathes of the green belt are granted protections despite offering limited beauty or recreational space. They want to home in on sites such as redundant petrol stations, car parks or wasteland, and reclassify them as “grey belt” land.

Opponents have claimed that high-quality green space is being sacrificed and reclassified as “grey”.

In order to meet their housing targets, councils are having to review their green belt land and bring it forward for housing. For example, Labour-led Leeds city council is preparing to reclassify up to 90pc of its green belt.

The Government has a further plan to tweak the definition of the grey belt. The current description lists a series of protected areas – such as heritage assets, sites of special scientific interest and irreplaceable habitats – which provide a strong reason to prevent the green belt from being designated “grey”.

But in changes put out for consultation, the MHCLG wants to delete this extensive list from its revised national planning framework, making it easier for councils to redesignate green belt land. It said the current definition creates “unnecessary” layers of additional protection.

There are fears this could loosen the protections granted to areas around churches or wetlands, but the Government has rejected the claims as “completely untrue”.

5. Green-belt villages at risk

In a move that raised eyebrows among planning experts, villages in England’s green belt can now be merged through housebuilding. Towns, however, are protected from urban sprawl, according to national planning policy.

The lack of protection for England’s quintessential village prompted concerns about mega-villages forming across the country. Fearful that vast amounts of land could be built on, Solihull Council rushed to reclassify four of its villages as towns to “maintain the integrity” of its green belt.

6. Twelve new towns

Twelve new towns comprising at least 10,000 new homes each are to be built in England over the coming decades.

The Bedfordshire village of Tempsford and the Cheshire village of Adlington are among the areas that have been put forward by the independent New Towns Taskforce as locations, although they are yet to be determined.

The latter’s inclusion attracted the ire of Tim Roca, the Labour MP, who said: “The countryside around Macclesfield is not an empty space waiting to be filled. Once it is gone, it is gone forever.”

Once the sites are confirmed, the Government hopes to “get spades in the ground” by 2029, yet they will face stiff opposition in the years to come, including from the National Farmers’ Union, which harbours “serious concerns” over the “significant impact” the towns will have on the countryside.

7. Biodiversity exemptions

More housing developments are to become exempt from rules requiring builders to improve wildlife habitats, in a move which campaigners fear will significantly impact the countryside. The draft changes will mean Biodiversity Net Gain (BNG) rules are loosened for small sites in an effort to speed up housebuilding.

Current BNG regulations – introduced just two years ago – mean developers must improve biodiversity on-site by 10pc, ensuring they leave the natural environment in a better state than before.

But ministers want to exempt small sites below 0.5 acres from these requirements. Countryside campaign groups fear the reforms will hurt out-of-town developments, as smaller-scale housing schemes are common in rural areas.

data.title: AI means NatWest is hiring a new breed of banker
data.description:
Young people should consider studying tech if they want a career in banking, the boss of NatWest has suggested.

Paul Thwaite, the chief executive, said banks were looking for “very different” graduates from those hired in the past amid the coming boom in artificial intelligence (AI).

He said data scientists, software engineers, architecture planners and “prompt engineers” – people who design instructions for AI tools – were the new breed of banker he was seeking to hire.

“The profile of graduates that we recruit today, and we’ll do in future years, is very different, for example, to what we were recruiting four or five years ago,” he said.

NatWest hires around 250 graduates every year, spread across tech, commercial banking, risk management and financial crime.

As a student, Thwaite studied management science and chemistry at the University of Manchester, then joined Santander’s graduate scheme before switching to NatWest.

In a sign of its growing tech focus, the bank has invested £1.2bn across the group in AI and simplification in an effort to cut costs.

AI is already being employed in areas such as customer service and it said using AI to handle complaints had led to saving around 90,000 hours per year.

Overall, the bank reported pre-tax profits of £7.7bn over the year, up a quarter from 2024 and the highest since before the financial crisis that forced NatWest into a government bailout. Shares in the bank were down by 1.8pc by mid-morning.

Mr Thwaite received £6.6m in pay over the year, up from £4.9m in 2024, making him the best-paid boss of the bank since 2010. NatWest also announced plans for a £750m share buyback programme.

NatWest unveiled a £2.7bn deal to buy wealth manager Evelyn Partners on Monday as it looks to diversify away from traditional lending.

The acquisition is the bank’s largest since its £49bn deal for ABN Amro in 2007, a takeover which precipitated its near collapse and state bailout during the financial crisis.

Shares in NatWest have fallen around 10pc since news of the acquisition, amid concerns that the bank might have overpaid.

However, Mr Thwaite said he believed that the drop in the share price was to do with the wider economic and political uncertainty.

“It’s been a pretty volatile week for a whole host of sectors. You had some of the political disruption at the start of the week and economic disruption, not just domestically, but also internationally,” he said.

“I’m not worried because it’s the acquisition of a strategic asset,” he added. “It’s very attractive from a growth perspective.”

data.title: Train lines ‘sabotaged’ near Winter Olympics
data.description:
Italian police suspect sabotage after three incidents including a fire on railways caused major delays for those travelling to the Winter Olympics.

Investigators have not ruled out arson carried out by anarchist groups, similar to what is thought to have happened in France at the Paris 2024 Olympics.

Saturday’s blaze, on the first full day of the games, was “presumably ‍deliberate” but had not been claimed, a spokesman for the police said.

Transport police and the anti-terrorist Digos force were investigating on site near the northern city of Bologna, the spokesperson said.

Local media reported that several cables were cut near Bologna’s central station, causing disruptions to high speed trains, cancellations and delays of up to 90 minutes.

Damage was reported on the Bologna-Padua line, where a crude explosive device was found. An electrical sub station caught fire on the Bologna-Ancona line in Pesaro, shortly before 6am on Saturday.

The fire hit a track switch and severed electrical cables used to detect train speed, causing delays ‍of ‌up to two hours for high-speed, Intercity and regional services at one of Italy’s busiest rail hubs.

The ‍fire targeted the line between Bologna and Venice, but also caused knock-on problems for traffic between Bologna and Milan, ⁠and ‌routes to ‍the Adriatic coast.

Milan is a co-host for the Winter Olympics alongside Cortina, which is accessible via train from Venice.

Italy’s state-owned railway Ferrovie dello Stato (FS) said that despite the disruption, trains were still running. 

Rail traffic was slowly returning to normal on Saturday.

The Italian ministry of transport said: “These actions of unprecedented gravity in no way tarnish Italy’s image in the world, which the games will make even more convincing and positive. 

It added that “the serious episodes of sabotage that occurred this morning near Bologna station and in Pesaro, which caused serious inconvenience to thousands of travellers, are worrying and are similar to the acts of terrorism that occurred in France a few hours after the opening ceremony of the Paris Olympic Games.”

At the 2024 Summer Olympic Games staged ⁠in Paris, saboteurs struck France’s TGV high-speed train network in ‌a series of pre-dawn ⁠attacks across the country, ‍causing travel chaos ahead of the opening ceremony.

Hundreds of thousands of passengers were affected by the disruption caused by what was called coordinated “acts of sabotage” by the then prime minister, Gabriel Attal.

Far-Left militants were blamed for the attack at the time. Iran denied being behind the sabotage after reports that Israel had warned France of an Iranian plot to derail the games.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Reeves accused of putting vital South West rail route at risk
data.description:
Rachel Reeves has been accused of putting South West England’s main rail route at risk by limiting maintenance spending while promising billions for Northern projects.

Fears are mounting that a sea cliff could collapse on to the line in Devon, cutting off Plymouth and the whole of Cornwall, after recent storms saw a sinkhole near the railway in Dawlish.

The cliff in question had been earmarked for £400m of earthworks after a 2014 collapse closed the line for eight weeks, costing the local economy £1.2bn.

However, the plan was abandoned after Labour sought to cut costs across the UK’s rail network. Instead, ministers suggested the cliff would be monitored for signs of movement. 

Storm Ingrid, which battered Devon two weeks ago, also saw waves destroy sections of a sea wall protecting the tracks at Dawlish, closing the line for two days.

Rebecca Smith, Tory MP for South West Devon, said the damage had stoked concerns that spending cuts had left the lifeline route exposed to potential disaster.

She said: “With all the tightening of the budget by the Chancellor, she has taken away contingency funding. So if there were something that needed an emergency reaction, the implication is that the money isn’t actually there.

“If you get the right kind of freakish storm, who knows what could happen. The question for me is, should this not be something that is in capital funding on a year-on-year basis?”

After Ms Smith asked in the Commons whether funding could be guaranteed for unplanned resilience work, James Murray, the chief secretary to the Treasury, said that budgets allow ministers to respond to emergencies.

He said: “All individual departments must recognise that unexpected pressures will come along; they need to prepare for them and make sure they have robust plans to respond.”

Ms Smith, a member of the transport select committee, said the Government’s reluctance to fund work on the cliff stood in contrast to its backing for the £45bn Northern Powerhouse Rail project, for which Ms Reeves last month announced £1bn of preparatory funding.

She said: “Why do they want to take a gamble when they are investing billions in other parts of the country? Why do we need a marginally faster rail system in the North when in the Southwest, if this went again, we’d be cut off for, I don’t know how long?”

Ms Smith also raised doubts about the level of monitoring that Network Rail is carrying out on the cliff, which is protected by the railway from the sea but prone to becoming sodden with surface water.

She said: “Are they monitoring it or just draining things? We need answers if they are not prepared to put the investment in to fix it.”

Martin Wrigley, Liberal Democrat MP for Newton Abbot, which includes Dawlish, said that had Storm Ingrid hit when seas were higher, the damage could have been much more severe.

He said the Department for Transport (DfT) should grant Network Rail money to undertake a study into a long-term solution for the cliff.

He said: “Monitoring is no good because the last collapse was a single, catastrophic failure, almost a tectonic shift of part of the cliff which came down on to the railway.”

Mr Wrigley said the damage to the Dawlish sea wall was also concerning, having affected areas on either side of a new section installed after the 2014 collapse.

The DfT said work carried out after the 2014 events held up well in the recent storms, with damage limited to a low wall between the coastal path and the railway. 

It said funding had been provided for a drainage trial and a range of advanced monitoring equipment, including tilt sensors that would provide a quick response if triggered.

Work will continue with Network Rail to assess the investment needed to ensure the line’s resilience and to develop any longer-term solutions.

data.title: Asda loses title of cheapest British supermarket to Tesco
data.description:
Asda is no longer the cheapest British supermarket as it loses the price war it started.

The Leeds-based grocer has fallen behind Tesco in a monthly ranking of the cost of a shop compiled by Which?.

The consumer champion ranked the major supermarkets based on both a small and large shop. In January, Asda fell behind Tesco on the cost of a small shop for the first time since September and remained behind German grocers Aldi and Lidl.

On the big shop, Asda lost the title of cheapest British supermarket to Tesco for the first time in a year. Aldi and Lidl were not included in this ranking because of their smaller ranges.

The findings are a blow to Allan Leighton, the Asda chairman, who launched a splashy price war in the supermarket sector last year in an effort to win back customers.

Mr Leighton boasted of a “war chest” to fund price cuts last March. He said the investment would result in a “material hit” to Asda’s profits but should help revive performance.

However, separate figures show the supermarket continues to lose ground. Asda’s sales fell by 3.7pc in the 12 weeks to Jan 25 compared with the same period a year earlier, according to Worldpanel figures.

Market share slipped from 12.3pc last year to 11.5pc today. However, in a small glimmer of hope, it rose from a record low of 11.4pc in the run up to Christmas.

Asda has struggled with fierce competition from Tesco, which has launched its own major price cutting drive. Last month, Ken Murphy, the Tesco chief executive, said the grocer “without a doubt, had responded very decisively to what Asda announced last year”.

Tesco recently brought back its blue and white stripe branding associated with its low-cost Value range and has been pushing through lower prices.

Worldpanel data showed Tesco gained its highest share of the grocery market in more than a decade over the Christmas period, at 28.7pc. Sales were up 4.4pc on last year.

Which? found that a trolley of 228 items would cost £1.45 more at Asda than Tesco when using a Clubcard. Without a Clubcard, Tesco would have been more expensive though more than 80pc of UK households are signed up to the supermarket’s loyalty scheme.

Asda has been cutting and outsourcing jobs as sales decline. It unveiled plans to slash more than 80 managers and dozens of warehouse staff last month.

Mr Leighton has already pushed through several rounds of job cuts since he took over at Asda in late 2024. Last year, a large group of in-store managers were made redundant, while hundreds of IT workers were axed following its botched IT upgrade.

Asda has blamed its recent poor sales on the IT upgrade, codenamed Project Future. Mr Leighton said in November it was “all to do with Future”.

A spokesman for Asda said: “Asda was the lowest‑priced supermarket every month in 2025 on the Which? big trolley comparison, and £50 cheaper in January on a like‑for‑like basket without loyalty pricing. Asda remains the best‑value supermarket for the big family shop with prices available to all.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Pandora to replace silver jewellery with platinum as prices go haywire
data.description:
Pandora plans to sell less silver jewellery and shift to platinum instead to shield itself from volatile prices.

The world’s largest jewellery maker, known for its charm bracelets, said it wanted to move away from silver following weeks of volatility in precious metal prices.

Berta de ‍Pablos-Barbier, Pandora’s new chief executive, said around 60pc of the company’s products were currently made of silver but it wanted to reduce that to “maybe 20pc in the end”.

Silver prices have swung wildly in recent weeks after Donald Trump’s unpredictable foreign policy prompted investors to flee into precious metals.

It was worth about £26 an ounce at the start of January before rocketing to a high of £88 an ounce by Jan 29, only to plummet to £58 an ounce in recent days.

This has proved a headache for Pandora because silver is used in the bulk of its products, although the company hedges about 70pc of its exposure to the precious metal.

Platinum has also seen prices rise and fall violently but the company hopes making more jewellery using the metal will help it diversify away from its reliance on silver.

Ms de ‍Pablos-Barbier said Pandora was “a jewellery brand, not a silver trader”.

Pandora on Wednesday warned it could face its first sales drop since the pandemic with the company predicting a small fall in organic sales at worst or just 2pc growth at best.

The company said its platinum jewellery was developed “to outperform silver in everyday wear” and had tested well with consumers.

Switching to platinum is likely to increase Pandora’s price tags significantly, with products sold by rival jewellers suggesting platinum jewellery can be twice as expensive as silver.

On Tiffany & Co, a narrow sterling silver ring is listed for £335 compared to £765 for a simple platinum wedding band.

Tarvier sells sterling silver rings for up to £74 while an equivalent platinum ring can cost up to £1,694.

In commodity markets, platinum trades for about £1,700 an ounce against around £58 an ounce for silver.

Pandora had already put prices up by 5pc in October, which it cited as the main driver behind an increased profitability gain in the last three months of 2025.

But the company warned that volatile gold and silver prices represented a “material headwind” to profit forecasts.

Standard Chartered analysts said precious metal volatility would likely continue “until there is greater certainty on the monetary policy outlook”.

data.title: BBC mast operator in crisis over terrestrial TV switch-off
data.description:
The company behind Britain’s terrestrial TV and radio network has been plunged into crisis amid uncertainty over a looming digital switchover.

The future of Arqiva, which operates around 1,500 masts used by the BBC and other broadcasters, has been thrown into doubt after its largest shareholder warned its investment in the company would likely be written down to zero.

In an update to the stock market this week, Digital 9 Infrastructure said its stake of almost 52pc in Arqiva is now thought to be worth nothing.

The writedown comes as the BBC and fellow public service broadcasters ITV and Channel 4 are locked in discussions with ministers about when terrestrial TV signals, delivered via Freeview, will be switched off and replaced by streaming.

The broadcasters are keen to push ahead with the switch-off, arguing that they face huge costs to maintain energy-intensive terrestrial signals as viewers increasingly shift to streaming.

Arqiva’s network of masts reaches 98.5pc of the UK population through Freeview.

It includes the 319-metre-tall, Grade II-listed Emley Moor transmitting station, which is the tallest freestanding structure in the UK, and Lancashire’s Winter Hill, which beams TV signals to more than 2.7 million homes in areas including Manchester and Liverpool.

Under current legislation, traditional TV is slated to continue until at least 2034. However, campaigners have called for this deadline to be extended, warning that a hasty switch-off could leave older and vulnerable audiences behind.

The campaign is heavily backed by Arqiva, which has a significant commercial interest in extending the life of traditional broadcast networks.

Exposure to ‘binary events’

Ministers this week kicked off a separate review into whether FM radio signals should also be switched off in the 2030s. The commercial radio industry had previously warned that the sector was being overlooked in discussions about switching off terrestrial masts.

Digital 9 acknowledged that Arqiva was “exposed to several binary events, including government policy and regulatory decisions”.

Digital 9’s stake in the business was worth £22.4m as of June 2025. Arqiva’s tumbling value had already been signalled in November when Australian investment giant Macquarie sold its 26.5pc stake for £16.5m, giving the company a valuation of just £62m.

Digital 9 has maxed out its debt facilities, raising questions over its ability to raise more money in the event of a future crisis.

A spokesman for Arqiva said Digital 9’s valuation of its business related to the “specific to the structure of their investment”.

The added: “Arqiva is a stable business with a healthy order book of existing business (£2.8bn as at 30 June 2025) and excellent targeted growth opportunities.”

Discussions over the future of terrestrial TV have proved to be politically fraught as officials in Whitehall seek to manage the transition to streaming without leaving audiences behind.

A report by Exeter University found that while 95pc of UK homes will use TV services delivered over the internet by 2040, 5pc will still rely on terrestrial TV. The report found that these people were more likely to be female, older and from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.

In addition to the switch-off, Arqiva is gearing up for the renewal of its broadcasting contract with the BBC, which is expected next year.

It comes as the BBC also enters negotiations over the future of the licence fee, with ministers considering alternative funding models including subscriptions and advertising.

The BBC is grappling with a squeeze on its budget following a freeze in licence fee income and as more households choose not to pay the levy. Corporation bosses yesterday told staff that further cost-cutting of up to £600m is now planned over the next three years.

While ITV and Channel 4 are commercially funded, they are also grappling with a drop in revenues as viewers defect to streaming rivals such as Netflix. ITV is in negotiations over a £1.6bn takeover by Sky, though talks have begun to slow, Reuters reported on Thursday.

Digital 9 warned that its stake is worthless because the value of the investment has fallen below the level of debt it took on to acquire the shareholding from the Canada Pension Plan. Digital 9 insisted there was a “credible pathway to value in certain scenarios”.

Shonil Chande, an analyst at Panmure Liberum, said the fact that Digital 9 had “substantially overpaid” for Arqiva was “no secret”.

data.title: BBC mast operator in crisis over terrestrial TV switch-off
data.description:
The company behind Britain’s terrestrial TV and radio network has been plunged into crisis amid uncertainty over a looming digital switchover.

The future of Arqiva, which operates around 1,500 masts used by the BBC and other broadcasters, has been thrown into doubt after its largest shareholder warned its investment in the company would likely be written down to zero.

In an update to the stock market this week, Digital 9 Infrastructure said its stake of almost 52pc in Arqiva is now thought to be worth nothing.

The writedown comes as the BBC and fellow public service broadcasters ITV and Channel 4 are locked in discussions with ministers about when terrestrial TV signals, delivered via Freeview, will be switched off and replaced by streaming.

The broadcasters are keen to push ahead with the switch-off, arguing that they face huge costs to maintain energy-intensive terrestrial signals as viewers increasingly shift to streaming.

Arqiva’s network of masts reaches 98.5pc of the UK population through Freeview.

It includes the 319-metre-tall, Grade II-listed Emley Moor transmitting station, which is the tallest freestanding structure in the UK, and Lancashire’s Winter Hill, which beams TV signals to more than 2.7 million homes in areas including Manchester and Liverpool.

Under current legislation, traditional TV is slated to continue until at least 2034. However, campaigners have called for this deadline to be extended, warning that a hasty switch-off could leave older and vulnerable audiences behind.

The campaign is heavily backed by Arqiva, which has a significant commercial interest in extending the life of traditional broadcast networks.

Exposure to ‘binary events’

Ministers this week kicked off a separate review into whether FM radio signals should also be switched off in the 2030s. The commercial radio industry had previously warned that the sector was being overlooked in discussions about switching off terrestrial masts.

Digital 9 acknowledged that Arqiva was “exposed to several binary events, including government policy and regulatory decisions”.

Digital 9’s stake in the business was worth £22.4m as of June 2025. Arqiva’s tumbling value had already been signalled in November when Australian investment giant Macquarie sold its 26.5pc stake for £16.5m, giving the company a valuation of just £62m.

Digital 9 has maxed out its debt facilities, raising questions over its ability to raise more money in the event of a future crisis.

A spokesman for Arqiva said Digital 9’s valuation of its business related to the “specific to the structure of their investment”.

The added: “Arqiva is a stable business with a healthy order book of existing business (£2.8bn as at 30 June 2025) and excellent targeted growth opportunities.”

Discussions over the future of terrestrial TV have proved to be politically fraught as officials in Whitehall seek to manage the transition to streaming without leaving audiences behind.

A report by Exeter University found that while 95pc of UK homes will use TV services delivered over the internet by 2040, 5pc will still rely on terrestrial TV. The report found that these people were more likely to be female, older and from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.

In addition to the switch-off, Arqiva is gearing up for the renewal of its broadcasting contract with the BBC, which is expected next year.

It comes as the BBC also enters negotiations over the future of the licence fee, with ministers considering alternative funding models including subscriptions and advertising.

The BBC is grappling with a squeeze on its budget following a freeze in licence fee income and as more households choose not to pay the levy. Corporation bosses yesterday told staff that further cost-cutting of up to £600m is now planned over the next three years.

While ITV and Channel 4 are commercially funded, they are also grappling with a drop in revenues as viewers defect to streaming rivals such as Netflix. ITV is in negotiations over a £1.6bn takeover by Sky, though talks have begun to slow, Reuters reported on Thursday.

Digital 9 warned that its stake is worthless because the value of the investment has fallen below the level of debt it took on to acquire the shareholding from the Canada Pension Plan. Digital 9 insisted there was a “credible pathway to value in certain scenarios”.

Shonil Chande, an analyst at Panmure Liberum, said the fact that Digital 9 had “substantially overpaid” for Arqiva was “no secret”.
