data.title: Heathrow boss: Airport isn’t crowded... foreigners just walk the wrong way
data.description:
The chief executive of Heathrow has said the airport is not crowded because foreigners just walk the wrong way.

Thomas Woldbye said people complain the airport is too busy when in fact people are just “crashing into each other”.

This is because European travellers are used to walking on the right, while the British walk on the left.

Mr Woldbye told an Aviation Club UK event on Thursday: “One of the reasons people think [Terminal 5] is crowded – it’s not, actually. But yes, people are in the wrong place.

“Problem is that all the British people keep to the left and normally Europeans keep to the right. And they do that in both directions. So we can be crashing into each other and I see that from personal experience.

“We just [need to] make sure that everybody going this way keeps going left, and [those] going this way keep to the right,” he added, gesturing with his hands to illustrate the different directions.

The humorous aside came after the Danish-born airport boss warned earlier this week that Heathrow could lose its coveted spot as Europe’s busiest airport to rivals such as Amsterdam or Paris unless a third runway is approved for construction.

Mr Woldbye said on Tuesday: “We remain Europe’s largest airport but latest figures show we may lose that position in 2026 and we cannot keep driving growth for the UK economy without more capacity.”

When asked what he thought of the Government’s position on the third runway, he said it was broadly supportive. But he added: “We don’t have long-term regulation, which is clear in terms of us building a business case.”

Last year, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, gave the go-ahead to Heathrow’s preferred plan to build a third runway over the top of the M25 motorway, a scheme likely to generate controversy over its cost and complexity.

The third runway scheme, which will see a 3,500-metre strip built to the north-west of Heathrow’s existing site, is currently estimated to cost around £49bn.

Included in the controversial plans are a scheme to build a 66,000-space car park at a cost of £2.6bn, as revealed by the City AM financial newspaper last summer.

The airport boss was also criticised on Thursday for raising drop-off fees paid by drivers to £7 – and threatening an £80 fine for motorists spending longer than 10 minutes saying goodbye to their loved ones.

He said in response to the criticism: “So when we make profits on all parts of the business, rather than just running the airport, that actually goes to reducing the charge [applied to passengers’ tickets].

“Whenever we make less money ... passengers are actually paying [for] that,” he added.

“We have an incentive to make as much money as we can, on the side, from anything that actually reduces the charge for passengers.”

Heathrow’s charges are regulated by the Government. The airport pays for its infrastructure by imposing a charge on airline passengers’ tickets.

Airlines, including Virgin Atlantic and British Airways, have set up a pressure group called Heathrow Reimagined, which calls for “urgent and fundamental reform” of this funding system.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ocado prepares to scrap 1,000 jobs in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Ocado is preparing to cut as many as 1,000 jobs as part of a scramble to drive down costs.

The struggling company, which employs around 20,000 staff, is preparing to reduce headcount to save hundreds of millions of pounds.

The proposed redundancies could be approved later this month, with those in its head office most likely to be affected. However, a final decision has yet to be made.

The job cuts are being made by Ocado Group, the London-listed company that builds robot warehouses and owns 50pc of online grocery business Ocado Retail.

It comes after a turbulent year for the business, which has seen its share price fall more than 40pc in the last six months alone.

This has been blamed on the loss of crucial warehouse contracts in North America.

A source close to the business claimed the proposed job losses form part of a strategy to improve cash flow over the next two years.

The company made a loss of £375m in 2024 but swung to a profit in the first half of last year on the revaluation of a stake in Ocado Retail, the groceries business it runs jointly with M&S.

Although best known for its grocery deliveries to British households, the company also pioneered the extensive use of robots in its warehouses, a technology it offers to businesses worldwide.

However, Ocado’s shares were rocked last year after US retailer Kroger confirmed plans to close three automated warehouses that used the company’s technology.

Kroger, which has built eight sites with Ocado since 2018, said it was moving to fulfil some of its online orders from existing stores instead of using the warehouses.

Another blow came last month when Sobeys, a supermarket in Canada, announced it would shut another site using the British company’s kit.

An Ocado spokesman said: “We regularly review our operations to ensure we’re set up for long-term success. If and when decisions are made that affect our people, we are committed to communicating with them directly and ensuring they are supported throughout.”

Ocado’s potential job losses, first reported by The Times, come at a time of darkening fortunes for those seeking work in the UK, fuelled by falling vacancies and rising redundancies, which have seen unemployment rise to a four-year high of 5.1pc.

Demand for staff fell in January for the 27th consecutive month, recruiters warned, with particularly large falls in vacancies for medical, secretarial, hospitality and retail workers.

The figures from the Recruitment and Employment Confederation and KPMG raise fears that unemployment will continue to rise, despite Government pledges to boost employment and wages.

“A growing, inclusive economy requires high levels of employment – a focus on encouraging firms to create jobs rather than discouraging that investment is more important than ever,” said Neil Carberry, chief executive of the REC.

“So far, the Government has struggled to convince businesses that it wants them to hire. That has to change in the decisions that are made this year if we are to avoid a continued rise in unemployment.”

A Government spokesman said: “This Government is creating the conditions businesses need to recruit and grow by investing £1.5bn in work, training and apprenticeships while restoring economic stability and bearing down on inflation, helping interest rates to fall six times, giving firms the confidence to hire.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BBC plots £600m of cuts with fears for jobs and programming
data.description:
The BBC plans to slash more than £500m from its annual budget in a move almost certain to involve job losses and programme cuts.

The public service broadcaster told staff it was aiming to strip out a tenth of its annual costs over the next three years. The BBC’s operating costs stood at just over £6bn last year, meaning the savings would amount to around £600m.

It is not yet clear exactly where the cost savings will be targeted but staff fear job cuts and further cutbacks to output.

Tim Davie, the outgoing director-general, said in a call with staff on Thursday that the cutbacks would affect all parts of the corporation, including its licence fee-funded public service arm and its commercial BBC Studios division.

In a note to staff, Mr Davie wrote: “To stay competitive and relevant, we need to adapt and innovate with purpose and pace.

“Over the next three years, like everybody in our sector, we will need to continue to find savings and move money to where we need it to remain relevant for our audiences.”

It is the latest in a series of demoralising rounds of cuts at the BBC, which had already pledged to strip out £700m by 2028.

The BBC has already wielded the axe in areas including radio and news, with BBC Two’s flagship evening news programme Newsnight slashed to half an hour and the HardTalk interview show scrapped altogether.

The corporation last year said it would spend £150m less on making new shows as it warned rising production costs had left it with an “unprecedented” funding challenge. In 2023, the BBC sparked outrage after cancelling daytime medical drama Doctors after 23 years.

The latest cuts were announced on the same day that a Florida judge ruled that Donald Trump’s $10bn (£7.3bn) case against the BBC will go to trial in February 2027, rejecting the broadcaster’s attempts to delay it.

A BBC spokesman said the cuts were unrelated to the case. They added: “It’s the reality of choices we have to make given ongoing funding pressures.”

The corporation is trying to plug a £500m hole in its finances, triggered by a combination of rising costs and a reduction in the number of Britons paying the licence fee.

The BBC’s licence fee income dropped by 30pc in real terms between 2010 and 2020 after a series of freezes and cuts to the levy. It has also suffered from a steady decline in licence fee payers, with a further 300,000 households ditching the fee last year.

The latest cut announcement came just a week after the Government confirmed that the cost of the licence fee will rise by £5.50 to £180 from April.

The funding crisis comes as the BBC enters into negotiations over the future of the licence fee ahead of the end of the current Charter period next year.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has said she is considering a range of possible reforms, including funding the broadcaster through advertising or subscriptions.

BBC bosses have acknowledged the need for reform but have pushed back against the idea of subscriptions or advertising, arguing that the broadcaster should remain a universal service.

The BBC is expected to submit its proposals for future funding in the coming weeks with other ideas reportedly under consideration including imposing a fee for its radio stations or extending the licence fee to include shows available to stream on services such as Netflix or ITVX.

The United States president’s defamation lawsuit is a further threat to the BBC’s fragile finances. The lawsuit is centred on a misleading edit of Mr Trump’s speech during the Jan 6 2021 riots in an episode of BBC One’s Panorama, which The Telegraph revealed.

The scandal prompted the resignation of both Mr Davie and Deborah Turness, the BBC’s head of news. The BBC has named senior executive Rhodri Talfan Davies as interim director-general, beginning in April, while it searches for a permanent replacement.

A BBC spokesman said: “Over the last three years, we have delivered more than a half a billion pounds worth of savings, much of which we’ve been able to reinvest into our output across the BBC.

“In a rapidly changing media market, we continue to face substantial financial pressures. As a result we expect to make further savings over the next three years of around 10pc of our costs.

“This is about the BBC becoming more productive and prioritising our offer to audiences to ensure we’re providing the best value for money, both now and in the future.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The towns where the taps ran dry
data.description:
On a grey Tuesday in East Grinstead, a little girl runs along the pavement to the redbrick town library. She stands at the automatic glass doors waiting for them to open, but nothing happens. A moment later her mother arrives. The woman looks at the sign that reads “library closed today”, then tells the child she will not be able to borrow any books after all.

Like almost every business or public building in this West Sussex town, the library is shut as a result of South East Water’s failure to keep the taps running.

The company’s bosses might just about be forgiven if this were a one-off. But it is the second officially declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent, still facing disruption after weeks of problems.

South East has repeatedly fallen short on its most basic duty. In 2023, 6,000 households in Kent and Sussex went without water when supplies at South East ran out. In 2022, thousands of people spent Christmas with no water due to leakages.

The company, which serves 2.3 million customers, is now well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from basket-case Thames Water, leaving its licence to operate under threat.

Businesses on the brink

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of South East Water, who took home £515,000 last year including a £115,000 bonus, was hauled in front of a parliamentary committee earlier this month. He had been summoned to answer questions on the crisis that hit Tunbridge Wells in December and ruined Christmas for 24,000 homes and businesses.

The spa town made an unlikely backdrop for a water crisis. From the 17th century, Tunbridge Wells’s mineral waters drew bathers and wealth.

But in the 21st century, water dealt an economic blow. Pub and bar owners in Tunbridge Wells told The Telegraph they had lost ruinous amounts of money.

“In four days we lost between £15,000 to £20,000. We are really struggling,” Bianca Omer, the manager of the cocktail bar Rocca said.

The bar survived. But Tudor Price, the chief executive of Kent’s chamber of commerce, believes that pubs, restaurants and bars in Tunbridge Wells “potentially lost about £20m of revenue” as a result of the outage.

Some are now on the brink. With many pubs already threatened by the Chancellor’s business rates raid, lengthy closure may deliver the final blow for some in Tunbridge Wells.

More alarmingly, a kidney treatment centre, 19 care homes, and two hospitals were left without water too.

‘Flying blind’

Debate has raged over whether the crisis in Tunbridge Wells was foreseeable. Hinton, perhaps unsurprisingly, reckons not.

At his parliamentary hearing he told Alistair Carmichael, the no-nonsense MP for Orkney and chairman of the environment select committee, that the whole thing was a bolt from the blue. In fact, because the crisis came as such a shock, Hinton insisted, he’d give himself “eight out of 10 in terms of response”.

The A-grade mark, as Carmichael put it, raised eyebrows among committee members. But when Marcus Rink, the boss of the Drinking Water Inspectorate, delivered his verdict, the benefit of the doubt evaporated.

“It shouldn’t have been a surprise,” Rink said. The water treatment works at nearby Pembury hadn’t just gone down for a few hours – it had been failing for some time, he explained.

“The start of the incident, in the opinion of my inspectors, was Nov 9.” With the Pembury plant the only water treatment works serving Tunbridge Wells, its continued good health is essential to the area.

Rink said South East was “flying blind”. The plant lacked electronic monitoring, relying on manual observations that missed failing filters.

Despite prior regulatory enforcement notices and missing micro-filtration units, the company had ignored weeks of visible deterioration, eventually leading to a totally avoidable system collapse.

The picture for South East became worse as Rink laid out more details. The company had been notified that there were issues with the treatment works a year previously. Testing had been intermittent, and the specialists monitoring the facility had all knocked off on Friday afternoon before returning on Monday morning. Nov 9 was a Sunday.

If MPs have been determined to hold Hinton to account, the same cannot be said of South East Water’s non-executive directors. Other managers have been sent out to face the music, among them a flustered Neil Hudson, South East’s head of water quality.

“What would happen to people if they do drink it?” a local BBC news reporter demanded to know after residents were advised to boil their water before using it.

“Any sort of normal, healthy individual will probably see no impact whatsoever. If anyone drinks the water and starts to feel unwell, our advice would be to speak to your GP,” Hudson said.

Asked by MPs why he had dodged the media, Hinton claimed journalists would not have asked relevant questions. They would have focused on his salary and South East Water’s finances, he complained.

When he finally came out of hiding, Hinton told ITV that he was “an operational CEO” and as such had spent the crisis participating in “traditional CEO activities”.

Henry Tuffnell, the young Labour MP for Pembrokeshire who sits on the environment committee, pointed out that for an “operational CEO” Hinton seemed to be remarkably short of operational detail.

Tuffnell tells The Telegraph: “You can’t say you were all over the detail, and therefore didn’t have time to do any media, if you weren’t over the detail.”

Even as Hinton faced MPs, back in Tunbridge Wells a second major incident was already in the offing. Still recovering from the pre-Christmas outage, locals were cut off again on Saturday.

Round two, according to South East, was due to “recent cold weather and a subsequent breakout of leaks and bursts”. This was no minor blip. Within days, 30,000 homes and businesses across Kent and Sussex had no water at all.

‘We have had enough’

In East Grinstead, four schoolboys kick a yellow football against the wall. They are the only people in the town who seem to think the outage is a good thing. “It’s a day off,” one of them offers cheerfully.

In Waitrose, almost all of the water has gone from the shelves except for a few bottles of San Pellegrino and some strawberry-flavoured Volvic. There was a water station in the car park the previous day, but it closed after 90 minutes.

East Grinstead council said: “We were not contacted directly by South East Water at any stage during the recent water supply disruption in the town. To the best of the council’s understanding, local knowledge was not utilised when setting up the initial water distribution stations.

“The town council regrets that it was not involved from the outset, as early engagement and local knowledge could have helped ensure a smoother, safer, and more coordinated response for the town’s residents.”

Local knowledge is clearly not South East Water’s strong suit. In December the company began its comedy of errors in Tunbridge Wells with the opening of a water station in Tonbridge, seven miles away.

For those affected, it’s hard to see the funny side. Gary, who lives homeless in East Grinstead, and had just two bottles of water left, told The Telegraph he was “scared”. Like many in the town, he wondered: “What is this all about?”

Jonathan Hawker, a Tunbridge Wells resident who set up the campaign group Dry Wells Action, says: “We can’t wash, flush toilets, do the dishes, wash clothes, top up boilers – all the normal stuff you take for granted – because of this abject failure by South East Water.

“We have had enough. We will now fight for our right to water. Tunbridge Wells is no longer disgusted. We are absolutely furious.”

Heavy hitters

South East’s board is now facing serious questions. Chris Train, the chairman, has been no more forthcoming about appearing publicly than his chief executive.

Described on the company’s website as bringing “a lot of experience”, he is also chairman of Sellafield, which in 2024 was fined for serious cyber security failings. The Commons public accounts committee criticised the nuclear power plant last year for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns”.

After Hinton’s select committee mauling, Train offered a review by one of the non-executive directors.

Committee chairman Alistair Carmichael responded: “Members of the press and public would be forgiven for seeing Mr Train’s announcement of a further review of the Pembury failure as an attempt to buy themselves time, to hunker down until this storm blows over.

“They also assert that their review will be ‘independent’. It stretches the meaning of ‘independent’ when their review is to be conducted by a member of its board, supported by its staff. What was the investigation done by the Drinking Water Inspectorate if it was not an independent review?”

Hinton’s furious phone call

Desperate Tunbridge Wells residents have called for the Army to be brought in. Though that may sound a little over the top, there is logic to the request.

Hawker says: “The Army are logistics specialists who are used to dealing with challenging situations. We need experts who can manage logistics in emergency situations.”

South East Water officials can’t even manage a basic task like carting bottles of water from one place to another, he argues.

If Hinton has been the baddie, Mike Martin, the Liberal Democrat MP for Tunbridge Wells, has emerged as something of a local hero. He has had his sleeves rolled up throughout the process and repeatedly taken Hinton to task. This resulted in Hinton phoning Martin one evening during the eye of the storm to give him both barrels.

In his evidence to the environment committee, Martin said Hinton had accused him of politicising the crisis, asking: “How can you sleep at night?” The chief executive admitted to Carmichael that the telephone tantrum had not been his “finest moment”.

South East is more used to getting away with it.

As well as the four major shortages customers have endured on Hinton’s watch, South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since Hinton took over. In 2023, it had the dubious accolade of recording the most interruptions and outages in England and Wales.

Its performance is getting worse, not better. In 2025, just 13 of it 38 annual targets were met – a little more than a third. Among the areas where it fell short were leakages, supply interruptions, water taste and odour, customer satisfaction and value for money. The previous two years, South East scored 15 – equivalent to less than 40pc. In 2022, it ranked positively on 24 of the 38 goals.

The company has suggested that its systems would be much better if only it had been able to raise customers’ bills, a familiar refrain in the water industry.

Yet it already has. Bills went up by 20pc just last year, meaning the average customer now pays £294 a year. It is also one of only five companies pushing to lift bills above the figure set by the regulator over the next five years.

Ofwat also gave short shrift to South East’s most recent investment plan. The company’s performance commitments were “of low to moderate ambition and do not go far enough in some cases”, the regulator said last April.

When it comes to “water supply interruptions, per capita consumption, repairs to burst mains and biodiversity”, the targets South East Water proposed “remained among the lowest across the sector”, Ofwat added.

South East responded by accusing the watchdog of being “unfair, disproportionate and punitive”. The company boasts of having “60 teams repairing leaks reactively and 86 leakage technicians and analysts searching for and finding leaks proactively”. More than 25,000 leaks were repaired in 2024, it says.

For any customers who feel they’re not getting value for money, the company points out: “The average drinking water bill now costs 81p per day, less than the cost of a second-class stamp.”

A spokesman said: “Our priority is improving South East Water in the future. We are strategically focused on our business plan and committed to delivering the infrastructure investment needed. This will improve our resilience as we face the challenges of climate change and population growth.”

It adds that Pembury’s water treatment works at the centre of the Tunbridge Wells crisis is due a full upgrade: “In advance of this full refurbishment, a number of improvements and changes are being made. Some of these are complete, and others are still ongoing, all of which are in line with the regulators’ timescales.”

Finances are underwater

South East’s financial performance is no less wretched than its operational performance. The company has failed to post a profit for the last three years, and though pre-tax losses roughly halved to £19.7m last year, they total £130m since 2022.

Weighed down with £1.3bn of borrowings – a figure that has steadily increased every year under Hinton – the cost of servicing those debts regularly eclipses the company’s profits. In the last five years, more than £400m has gone out of the door on debt costs and finance charges alone.

A further £168m has been paid out dividends, £136m of it in the form of a regulator-approved repayment of an internal loan to its parent company in 2020. South East Water is ultimately owned by a consortium comprising Australian and Canadian funds and NatWest’s pension fund.

In the two-year period from 2020 to 2022, the amount that South East paid out in dividends and debt interest surpassed what it spent upgrading infrastructure.

In 2024, the company revealed that it needed further cash to stay afloat. Ten months later, it received a £200m equity injection from shareholders.

Yet none of this has stopped Hinton receiving hundreds of thousands of pounds in pay and bonuses every year. Total earnings since assuming the top job in 2020 stand at an estimated £2.3m. Last year, he was paid a basic salary of £307,000, a £115,000 bonus and received a further £35,000 in pension contributions and other benefits.

A spokesman for South East said: “South East Water remains committed to a remuneration framework that supports a performance culture, and recognises success but does not reward poor performance.

“The bonus scheme is evaluated by the company’s independent non-executive director-led remuneration committee. The executive directors are not part of this committee and have no say in their remuneration.

“We can confirm that no bonus payment was made for operational performance for the year 2024-25. Details of all payments made can be found in our annual report on our website.”

‘Out of control’

Yet pinning the fiasco on a lack of investment when executives’ backs are against the wall doesn’t wash with Tunbridge Wells MP Mike Martin.

“Quite clearly, corporate governance has broken down,” he said. “We are now in a situation where a major public utility is out of control – failing its customers, risking public health and treating Parliament with contempt.”

If the board won’t step up, Martin thinks the utility’s bosses have to get involved. NatWest’s pension scheme owns 25pc of South East Water.

“NatWest Group Pension Fund will use its influence as a minority shareholder to direct South East Water’s board to ensure these issues are fully resolved,” it said in response to a letter from Martin this week.

The regulator has been spurred to action too. On Wednesday evening, the Government finally showed some mettle by calling on Ofwat to review South East Water’s licence to operate.

In an uncharacteristic show of speediness, on Thursday Ofwat announced an investigation into whether the supplier had met its obligations and offered support to those affected. Ofwat sceptics point out the regulator has been investigating South East since 2023.

There are local suspicions that Labour have, to a degree, let the crisis escalate because Tunbridge Wells and East Grinstead will never turn red. Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform UK, told The Telegraph that’s overthinking it. He said: “The problem is utter management incompetence at South East Water. My hunch is cock-up, not conspiracy.”

Yet the crisis is ongoing, and the inquisition is just getting started. By the water station at East Grinstead sports club, where the cars are backed up as far as you can see and people are pushing shopping trolleys full of bottles back home, a little boy on a bike asks if you can drink the water from the pond.

data.title: The City has become America’s plaything
data.description:
It is a sad yet undeniable fact that the City of London is suffering from serious decline.

It once acted as a giant beacon for trade and investment but the Square Mile has today lost much of its allure.

The financial crisis, overly burdensome regulation, a shrinking stock market, a shift in working habits and massive technological change have all contributed to the malaise.

The question that City officials grapple with is whether it is terminal or not.

Senior figures in the financial world point out that the City has faced repeated challenges during its long history – including several plagues, the Great Fire of London and the Blitz – and has managed to bounce back stronger every time.

Yet it is hard to escape the sense that this time is different, that what we are witnessing is the capital being slowly hollowed out until it represents a husk of its former self.

The news that fund giant Schroders has succumbed to a £10bn takeover at the hands of a bigger US rival must rank as one of the most crippling blows the City has suffered yet. In fact, it may be the death knell for London’s status as one of the truly global financial centres.

As if that wasn’t sufficiently humiliating, our once-great financial district is in danger of being turned into America’s plaything.

Bigger, bolder, more productive US companies – backed by a president fully behind them – are swooping on their weaker, meeker British counterparts with abandon.

The UK boasts a long list of stored financial institutions but few are as venerable as Schroders.

Indeed it is hard to think of a greater symbol of Britain’s sorry demise than the sight of the blue-blooded money manager – a business that was founded a year before the Battle of Trafalgar – surrendering more than 200 years of independence and family ownership to an opportunistic trans-Atlantic interloper without so much as a peep of protest or fight.

That the deal came on the same day the UK economy missed its growth forecasts with yet another thoroughly dismal set of results is telling.

It is not a coincidence that London’s inexorable slump comes as Britain’s place in the world becomes more irrelevant. The fate of both are inextricably linked. As London bleeds companies, corporate chiefs and capital, the UK is on course for its worst performance in a century.

Many will say the sale of Schroders is somehow a demonstration of Britain’s great strength and confidence in its prospects. This has long been the narrative that we have all been fed as our best and brightest companies have fallen into foreign hands.

Don’t be fooled by the spin: prosperous countries don’t allow their biggest assets to be sold to the highest bidder in the same way that businesses with strong, independent futures don’t flop into the arms of an admirer at the first opportunity.

Dame Elizabeth Corley, the chairman of Schroders, insists that despite quitting the stock market, London would “remain at the heart of this enlarged business”, serving as the combined group’s non-US headquarters.

Meanwhile Richard Oldfield, the company’s chief executive, deserves a medal for his attempts to talk up the sale.

“If we increase growth, London does even better than it would have done on a standalone basis,” he said, ignoring the fact that its new owner Nuveen – and Chicago, America’s third most populous city, where the company is headquartered – surely stand to benefit far more.

It is all desperately hopeful to the point of naivety. Ambitious and assertive American bosses know weakness when they see it, which is why Schroders has found itself in the crosshairs.

There is no getting away from the sense that Schroders is a fading force.

Ten months ago, Schroders shares were trading at a decade low and while the share price has staged a decent recovery, the reluctance of investors to pay a premium for market expertise when active funds are consistently being outperformed by passive competitors at a fraction of the cost has the makings of an existential crisis.

Similarly, while the Schroders family and the company’s board are having their tummies tickled, UK boardrooms are under siege from across the pond.

Arguably more concerning than the prospect of British fund management royalty vanishing is the arrival of activist fund Elliott on the shareholder register of the London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG).

This too has been greeted by something of “nothing to see here” response despite a 35pc fall in LSEG’s share price amid the threat from artificial intelligence, as well as a prolonged slump in share listings.

To read the reaction from some quarters, you would think Elliott’s approach was nothing more than a misunderstanding.

The suggestion seems to be that once it gains a better appreciation of LSEG’s evolving business model, one of Wall Street’s most feared activists will quietly sell its shares and vanish into the night. Such an assumption would most probably prove to be sorely misguided. Elliott’s campaigns usually result in some sort of shake-up even when the target resists.

No less damning of how complacent the City has become is the attack launched on multiple underperforming UK investment trusts by Boaz Weinstein, the US raider.

There are undoubtedly aspects of his assault that don’t necessarily stack up. Having adopted a scattergun approach that captures no less than seven trusts, Weinstein can have few complaints about those who paint him as a smash-and-grab merchant.

Equally, it is fair to question his chances of improving the fortunes of such a disparate bunch of firms.

However, there is more than a ring of truth to his accusation that sector performance has ranged from “underwhelming” to “disastrous”.

Even Terry Smith, the Mauritius-based fund manager who is no stranger to a dust-up, admitted that Weinstein had a point about the need for change at his Smithson trust.

Yet Weinstein’s remarks also read like an epitaph of Britain more generally as predatory American investors pounce en masse.

data.title: Miliband plots solar farms in space in quest to hit net zero
data.description:
Solar farms could be deployed in space to help Britain hit net zero targets, according to a new report published by the Energy Department.

Under ambitious clean power plans, orbiting solar satellites could be used to capture the sun’s energy before beaming it down to an antenna on Earth to be converted into electricity.

The proposal has been cited in a new government study on the latest space technologies which suggests that solar panels could be installed on satellites on Earth before being launched into space.

It is perhaps the most radical proposal yet as Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, strives to achieve net zero by 2050.

“Space-based solar power could provide Great Britain with constant, predictable, zero-carbon power at the GW [gigawatt] scale that displaces both intermittent renewables and fossil fuel generation,” the report said.

“A space-based power system involves one or multiple satellites equipped with lightweight solar panels capturing the sun’s energy in space.

“The satellite generates electricity, converts it into microwave radiation and beams it to a ground-based rectifying antenna (rectenna). The rectenna converts the microwave energy into electricity.”

Such ideas have been around for decades – but mainly in science fiction.

Isaac Asimov is credited with the first mention of the idea in a 1941 short story featuring a space station that collects energy and beams it to Earth.

Other science fiction authors have described similar ideas. Arthur C Clarke’s 1962 book Profiles of the Future discussed wireless transmission of power from space-based solar farms.

In recent years, several major space agencies and private firms have moved beyond theoretical papers into hardware testing and orbital demonstrations. These include the UK Space Agency and UK start-ups such as Space Solar.

The report was commissioned in 2021 by Kwasi Kwarteng, then the Conservative business secretary, whose brief included leading on net zero strategies and space technology.

He commissioned the Frazer-Nash consultancy to investigate the idea, with the final report finally published this week by the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero.

It suggests that although initial costs would be high, they would plummet as the technology matured, potentially falling to between £87 per megawatt hour (MWh) and £129 per MWh by 2040.

This compares with a predicted cost of £150 per MWh for new nuclear. The latest offshore wind contracts saw operators offered about £90 per MWh for fixed turbines and £216 per MWh for floating wind.

This suggests that space-based power station operators could be given contracts for difference, giving them a guaranteed minimum price for the power generated. This is the same subsidy scheme used to support renewables such as wind.

However, the report warns that the UK Government would have to help fund initial development, suggesting that the best approach would be to fund a smaller system that could be scaled up later once investors had gained confidence.

“Without prior de-risking, the scale of the upfront investment required for the first large-scale system may deter investors, making it more difficult to unlock the full benefits of space-based power,” the report said.

Such ideas have gained traction with space agencies and other researchers. The European Space Agency (ESA) is currently in the middle of a multi-year research and development phase called Solaris, with a demonstrative satellite in planning.

China has launched its own project with plans for a GW-scale power station in orbit by 2050.

In the UK, start-up Space Solar has been working with the UK Space Agency to design self-assembling orbiting solar power stations.

“Our work has continued to demonstrate the viability of space-based solar power as a firm, uninterrupted clean power source, ready to be deployed on a commercial scale,” the company said recently.

data.title: NHS to pay £3bn more for drugs to escape Trump tariffs
data.description:
The NHS has agreed to pay 25pc more for new medicines to escape Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs on drugs.

The US president unveiled a new pharmaceutical trade deal with Britain on Monday, striking an agreement to impose zero import tariffs on medical products from the UK.

It will see British medicines carved out from sweeping new levies, offering a major boost for one of Britain’s biggest exports to America. In 2024, the UK exported around £6.6bn in pharmaceutical and medical products to the US.

The agreement includes a pledge for the NHS to increase the net price it pays for new medicines by 25pc, which will add around £3bn to the taxpayer’s £12bn annual medical spending bill. This will be phased in.

It follows weeks of uncertainty over the future of the industry. In August, the US president threatened to impose tariffs of up to 250pc on all pharmaceutical imports – including the UK.

In September, he said there would be 100pc tariffs on “any branded or patented Pharmaceutical Product” coming into the US from October 1. At the time, he said companies could get an exemption if they had started building factories in the US.

While the White House has agreed to a spate of country-specific trade deals over the past year, pharma tariffs are expected to fall outside the scope of these agreements.

Under the new agreement, the NHS has also committed to slashing rebates that drug companies have to pay on sales of newer medicines to the health service.

The so-called Voluntary Scheme for Branded Medicines Pricing, Access and Growth (VPAG) is designed to cap the overall NHS drug bill but pharmaceutical companies have complained it has failed to keep pace with inflation.

Additionally, the deal means the UK will cut the rebate rate from 22.9pc to 15pc in 2026.

It is also understood that the Government will increase the threshold that determines which treatments are available on the NHS, boosting the spending cap on certain medicines and paving the way for more cutting-edge treatments to be introduced.

Drug giants have long complained over the decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money, claiming this does not allow them to get the best new drugs into the NHS.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said: “For too long, American patients have been forced to subsidise prescription drugs and biologics in other developed countries by paying a significant premium for the same products in ours.

“Today, the United States and the United Kingdom announce this negotiated outcome pricing for innovative pharmaceuticals, which will help drive investment and innovation in both countries.”

He added: “The Trump Administration is reviewing the pharmaceutical pricing practices of many other US trading partners and hopes that they will follow suit with constructive negotiations.”

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, said the deal would bring new jobs to America.

Mr Lutnick said: “This deal doesn’t just deepen our economic partnership with the United Kingdom – it ensures that the breakthroughs of tomorrow will be built, tested and produced on American soil.”

The White House has previously made clear its frustration over the lack of spending on innovative medicines in Britain. Currently, the UK spends just 0.28pc of its GDP on new medicines, roughly a third of what America does.

Britain previously pledged to review the health service’s drug pricing to take into account “the concerns of the president” under the UK-US trade deal struck in May.

Mr Trump has called for an end to overseas countries “freeloading” on American innovation, singling out Britain for a place where medicine prices are unfairly low compared to the US.

The US ambassador last month said Britain needed to change how it priced medicines or risk more global drug companies scrapping investments in the UK.

Merck, one of America’s largest drug companies, this year ditched plans to open a £1bn research centre in London, while AstraZeneca has paused £200m spending on its Cambridge research facility.

Liz Kendall, the Science and Technology Secretary, said the deal would “enable and incentivise life sciences companies to continue to invest and innovate right here in the UK”.

Chris Boerner, chief executive of Bristol Myers Squibb, said the trade agreement meant the pharmaceutical company will invest “upwards of $500m (£378m)” over the next five years.

Mr Boerner said: “This investment will come across multiple areas of the business including research, development and manufacturing. This agreement is a sign of progress and one that creates an environment conducive to our continued presence in the UK.”

data.title: Shell chief exec to become one of FTSE’s best paid bosses
data.description:
Shell’s chief executive is on course to become one of the best-paid executives on the London Stock Exchange as his company’s move away from green energy pays off.

Wael Sawan could see his total pay packet jump by £4.5m to a maximum £19m a year under proposals drawn up by the FTSE 100 company. That would make him one of the country’s highest paid blue chip executives.

Other high-earners include AstraZeneca’s Pascal Soriot, who was paid £15m in 2024, and Rolls-Royce’s Tufan Erginbilgic, whose pay is reportedly being increased to a maximum of £18m. Simon Peckham, Melrose’s chief executive, took home £58m upon retiring last year.

Mr Sawan’s base salary is just over £1.5m and the changes would mean he could earn nine times that amount per year in long-term performance pay, up from a maximum of six times currently.

That would make him eligible to receive stock awards worth £13.8m, up from £9m previously. On top of that, he can receive a maximum annual bonus of £3.8m – taking his total potential package to more than £19m.

Under Sawan, Shell has cut its exposure to green energy and focused its attention on more profitable oil and gas operations.

In November, Shell confirmed it was ditching its only two UK wind farm projects – MarramWind farm and CampionWind off the east coast of Scotland – after carrying out a business review.

Earlier in 2025, the company also revealed plans to ramp up gas sales while reducing the share of wind and solar in its power generation portfolio from 50pc to 20pc by 2030.

Shell is focusing investment in gas-fired power plants and grid-scale batteries instead, with bosses also planning to maintain oil and gas output at current levels through the end of the decade. Investors have cheered the focus on fossil fuels.

The company is already the world’s largest producer of liquefied natural gas (LNG).

Shares in the company have jumped 22pc since Mr Sawan took over in January 2023. That compares to a 0.1pc increase at BP, a 33pc increase at ExxonMobil and a 1.2pc increase at Chevron over the same period.

Mr Sawan’s proposed pay deal would still be far smaller than those of his US rivals. Darren Woods, the boss of Exxon, received $44.1m (£32.2m) last year, while Chevron boss Mike Wirth made $32.7m.

On Wednesday, a Shell spokesman said: “Every three years, Shell seeks shareholder approval for a new executive director remuneration policy as a standard part of regulations for UK-listed companies. 

“The last vote was in 2023, so this is part of the usual cycle. Final proposals will be published in the 2025 annual report on March 12 and voted on at the annual general meeting.”

data.title: Britain’s energy suppliers are still in a worryingly shaky state
data.description:
Four years on from the energy crisis that triggered the collapse of around half of UK suppliers, is our power system more secure?

You might think the answer is yes, after Ofgem introduced new rules intended to make sure energy companies are more financially resilient should future shocks occur.

But the energy regulator has confirmed that five suppliers are failing to meet its new capital requirements, up from three last year.

It has not named the suppliers. However, both Octopus Energy and Ovo Energy have both publicly confirmed they are not meeting the requirements. Both companies have recently agreed to capitalisation plans to help them meet the target.

Much smaller supplier Utilita also reported difficulties meeting the target due to seasonal demand fluctuations, but said it was short-lived and they soon went back above the target.

Under rules introduced in March 2025, suppliers are now expected to hold net assets, excluding intangibles such as goodwill, of £115 for every dual fuel customer they have, and half that sum for every customer on an individual fuel contract.

The logic of setting this spare cash aside is simple: suppliers should have enough balance sheet resilience to survive shocks in wholesale gas markets without failing and leaving consumers to pick up the tab.

Significant costs are incurred when a supplier fails, many of which are recovered through a levy on all energy bills.

In 2021-22, when half the suppliers failed, these costs ran into the billions and were loaded onto bills at a time when households were already struggling with high energy prices.

The biggest cost arises from credit balances: if a customer is in credit with a supplier that fails, the new energy company that takes on that customer must honour that balance. The “lifeboat” supplier is reimbursed for that cost through a levy on all our bills.

Another major cost is hedging – any gas or electricity purchased in advance by the failed supplier is treated as an asset in insolvency and used to repay creditors.

It cannot legally be transferred to the supplier that takes on the customers, meaning they have to go and buy contracts to cover themselves. That is another expense added to the levy.

The regime has not been without controversy. Centrica, owner of British Gas, has argued that suppliers should ring-fence customer credit balances so they are not mixed with the supplier’s own money and used to fund day-to-day operations.

Most other suppliers lobbied hard against it, and Ofgem ultimately declined to mandate ring-fencing, discouraging the use of credit balances as working capital without requiring a hard separation.

Ofgem sets out a range of restrictions it can impose on suppliers that are not on track to meet the requirement. In serious cases, it can require full ring-fencing of customer balances and restrict new customer acquisition.

The regulator does not name the suppliers with capitalisation plans nor detail anything about the plans themselves and progress towards meeting them.

The logic is that publicly naming them could create a “run on the bank” situation, where customers quit for fear of losing their power supply, (even though the regulatory regime ensures this would not happen even in the event of a failure).

This lack of transparency is increasingly hard to defend given the size of the two suppliers we know to be on the list. Octopus is the UK’s biggest supplier, with more than seven million customers, and Ovo has around four million customers.

There is no suggestion that either business is in financial trouble or at risk of going bust, but the scale of these businesses means serious thought must be given to the possibility.

Both are far bigger than Bulb, which had 1.7 million customers when it collapsed in November 2021. Bulb was considered too large for a conventional supplier of last resort process and required an expensive special administration regime to ensure customers were not left without power.

In banking, capital ratios and risk-weighted assets of major firms are published every quarter by the Bank of England to maintain confidence. Why not in the energy sector too?

Energy consumers are exposed in a similar way: their credit balances are effectively guaranteed, just as deposits are protected under the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. 

If we accept that supplier failures impose system-wide costs, then transparency should be a core part of the prudential regime, not an optional extra.

The primary role of suppliers is to handle customer money and do some accounting, which is essentially what retail banks do. 

If Ofgem’s experiment in regulation fails, it will be hard to defend its continued responsibility for the retail supply market.

data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Builder denies affair cost him slice of mother’s £2.5m fortune
data.description:
A builder was all but excluded from his late mother’s fortune partly because she was “ashamed” of him for cheating on his wife, a court has heard.

Gary MacDougall, 70, had expected to split Jeanne MacDougall’s estate – valued at £2.5m – with his sister Sandra Thomas, 65, and her husband, Lloyd, after she died in April 2020.

But because of a change to her will in 2011 and a series of property sales and gifts, Mr MacDougall is likely to be left with “nil”, the High Court was told.

He is now suing at the High Court to overturn his mother’s last will, while also accusing his sister and brother-in-law of plundering her fortune while she was still alive but incapable of consent.

At a hearing this week, he was forced to deny that his mother had genuine reasons to cut him out, including her shame over him cheating on his wife, Anna, with another woman.

Alexander Learmonth KC, for the Thomases, told the court there were valid reasons why Mrs MacDougall had changed her will in favour of her daughter and son-in-law.

He said the couple had cared for her in her old age, whereas Mr MacDougall, who had worked with his mother in the family business, had a “fractious” relationship with her.

He told the court: “Like all testators, Jeanne clearly had a variety of reasons for wanting to make a new will in the terms she did.

“To some extent, it would be speculation as to what they were, and the court is not required to indulge in that, but they certainly included a sense of how well-off Gary had become, and gratitude for the time and care given her by [Lloyd] and Sandra.

“They may also have included growing irritation with Gary, whether that be his marital infidelity, sharp words in the office, infrequency of visits or otherwise. It certainly cannot be said that the 2011 will is in any way irrational.”

Cross-examining Mr MacDougall, the barrister suggested that his mother, as a member of an older, more traditional generation, was “disappointed” in her son about his affair in 2008.

“Your mother, being of the older generation and being fond of your wife, was not happy with you, was she?” he put to Mr MacDougall. “She was very disappointed with what you had done.”

Mr MacDougall replied: “She wasn’t happy, no – she was delighted when it concluded.

“She reminded me of my responsibilities, but it was a very brief fling. If she was cross with me, Mum would have let me know. She would have given me both barrels.”

Mr Learmonth claimed that Mrs MacDougall, when making her will, was also conscious of the benefits her son had already received, having ultimately become sole owner of the family business.

He had received “huge assets” and now has “a considerable portfolio of rental properties, as well as a holiday home in Cyprus and an oast house in the countryside,” he said.

Mr MacDougall insisted that his success was down to him “grafting” while his sister and brother-in-law lived “the life of luxury”, and added: “Where’s the reward?”

Family fortune built up in property

Judge Nicola Rushton KC heard the MacDougall family fortune derived from the “substantial real estate portfolio” built up ty the siblings’ father Alec, a property developer.

Harry Martin, Mr MacDougall’s barrister, said that over the years it had been made clear to the two siblings by their parents that they would ultimately receive “broadly equal financial treatment and inheritance”.

After their father’s death, the siblings’ mother made a will in 2008, which Mr Martin said amounted to a “broadly equal” split between her son and his family and her daughter and son-in-law.

But another will was then made in 2011, under which all of her four properties went to Mrs Thomas and her husband, and they received the majority of her savings.

Mr MacDougall and his sister would split the small amount that was left, but after the costs and expenses of estate administration, that is “likely to be worth nil”, the court was told.

Mr MacDougall claims the will is invalid because of “presumed undue influence” having been made at a time when his mother was elderly and dependent on his sister and brother-in-law.

‘More than £2m left bank accounts’

He is also laying claim to a share of about £1.7m of his mother’s money which he says was “misappropriated” from her bank accounts before she died, and spent by his sister and brother-in-law on themselves and their family.

Mr Martin said more than £2m left Mrs MacDougall’s bank accounts between 2012 and 2020, with less than £500,000 being attributed to spending for her own benefit.

Alleged expenditure by Mrs Thomas and her husband included “paying for their daughter’s wedding at the five-star Savoy Hotel in London, shopping on Oxford Street, flights, spending on holiday in Spain, £30,000 to the car manufacturer Jaguar and meals out at The Ivy”, the barrister said.

Mr Learmonth accepted Mrs Thomas and her husband exceeded their authority under the power of attorney, but did not do so deliberately, having misunderstood what they were entitled to do.

The case continues.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The battle over ‘mad’ plans to redevelop Liverpool Street station
data.description:
On a cold night this week as the rain lashed down, campaigners gathered in an unassuming east London office block to thwart a £1.5bn revamp of Britain’s busiest station.

The Liverpool Street Station Campaign (Lissca) – a “supergroup” of heritage enthusiasts spearheaded by TV presenter Griff Rhys Jones – is fighting plans to demolish and rebuild key parts of the railway terminus and linked Underground station.

The group this week unveiled its alternative vision, one they claim will help to modernise Liverpool Street station without destroying its history.

The rival plan drawn up by John McAslan + Partners, the architects behind the transformation of King’s Cross station in 2012, will be presented to local officials in the coming weeks.

“If you’re coming from Italy on a train to Liverpool Street station, would you rather see a brand new gateway ... or something which says Victorian, Britain and great railway heritage?” Rhys Jones says.

“If you break it, it’s gone, you can’t get it back. It could be a much richer experience, without doing an awful lot.”

Network Rail say expansion is needed to cope with the expected increase in passenger numbers at a station already at capacity. They argue only a wholesale overhaul will do.

Campaigners, meanwhile, claim that plans to modernise the station would ruin a Victorian landmark and create years of needless disruption.

The fight over Liverpool Street station, which opened fully in 1875, is a microcosm of the tug of war between heritage and modernisation that threatens to hamper Labour’s ambitions for a construction boom.

Since taking power, Sir Keir Starmer has vowed to make it more difficult for people to oppose major building projects.

He has pledged to override the “whims of Nimbys” and back the builders rather than the “blockers”. But in cases like Liverpool Street, these statements have only galvanised the opposition.

Al Watson, head of planning at Taylor Wessing, says: “The situation is more tense now than it was four years ago because the previous government leaned more towards heritage.

“This Government, by its very nature, pre- and post-election, is pro-development. That’s the vision, that’s the instruction, and that’s why it is more tense now than it has been in a long time.”

Until now, Lissca has faced down each proposal to redevelop Liverpool Street station. The group in fact dates back to the 1970s, when British Rail’s initial bid to demolish the terminus was thwarted by Lissca, then fronted by the poet Sir John Betjeman.

More recently, plans drawn up in 2023 by architects Herzog and de Meuron were shot down after the group helped mobilise 2,200 objections and 26,000 petition signatures.

“We’ve won battle after battle,” says Sir Simon Jenkins, founder of the Railway Heritage Trust and a supporter of the group.

The new plans, ordered by Network Rail and designed by architects Acme, will involve partially demolishing the station to expand its concourse.

A 19-storey, 97m glass office tower would be built above a neighbouring listed former hotel, while gothic yellow brick vaults would arch over the station’s entrances.

Over 150 million passengers by 2041

Network Rail argues that expansion is essential.

The opening of the Elizabeth Line, which passes through Liverpool Street, has helped the station become the UK’s busiest in each of the past three years.

There were 98 million rail entries and exits last year, and Network Rail predicts this will grow to 158 million by 2041.

Planning officials for the City of London Corporation are currently reviewing the revamp proposals, with councillors expected to vote on them on Feb 10.

The scheme has so far attracted more than 2,275 letters of objection and 1,135 in favour.

The February deadline has galvanised Lissca into action, with the group hoping their alternative plans will convince the City of London to reject Network Rail’s plans.

“What we have to do over the next 20 days … is do what we can to make sure that [the committee is] aware that the City of London deserves better than this current scheme that is before them, and they have the right to ask for it to be better,” Jones says.

‘Illogical’ plans

While Network Rail’s designs will evoke the station’s Victorian heritage, Lissca believe they can retain far more of the original building.

They estimates its plans will take half the time and cost half as much as Network Rail’s proposals, with almost no demolition required.

“The reason it’s so high is because of the void costs, created by the demolition of so much fabric,” says McAslan. “It’s so illogical, it’s mad.”

Lissca’s vision includes a green walkway inspired by the Eden Project, with Sir Tim Smit, founder of the Eden Project, advising McAslan on landscape strategy.

A vaulted office building would sit at the northern end of the station’s platforms, with nine floors of timber hanging from a lightweight steel frame to provide around 700,000 sq ft of workspace.

McAslan believes Network Rail’s plans will cause 12 years of disruption for travellers – a timeframe he calls “completely unacceptable”.

But Network Rail says this timeframe is a gross exaggeration.

Work on modernising the station itself will begin as early as 2028 and be completed in about five years, with development of offices above the passenger concourse taking three more.

While ticket barriers will be moved and shops demolished to enlarge the concourse, the impact on people using the station will be limited to the “mild inconvenience” of having to use different entrances at certain times, Network Rail says.

Officials argue the project should be no more disruptive than the modernisation of Paddington at the turn of the century and far removed from the upheaval that accompanied the upgrade of London Bridge station.

The five-year, £1bn project to transform London Bridge, the capital’s oldest rail terminus, resulted in delays and dangerous levels of overcrowding.

A Network Rail spokesman said: “It’s very much not going to take 12 years and it won’t be on the scale of London Bridge because none of the work being done is to create new tracks and platforms.”

Network Rail commissioned an independent team to review McAslan’s preliminary scheme but concluded it was undeliverable owing to complexity, funding challenges and the potential for prolonged station closures.

Its review concluded that building offices over the train shed – as proposed in the McAslan plan – would be too costly and structurally challenging because Network Rail does not own land to either side.

It would also require “significant closure” of the station to build a deck over live lines.

Back in the war room at McAslan’s Farringdon offices, campaigners are undeterred.

“There’s a critical mass of buildings which create a sense of the Victorian past in that part of the city, and it must be preserved,” says Sir Simon.

“Just remember, there’s always a choice. Nothing is inevitable. Fight on.”

data.title: Schroders to quit London stock market in £9.9bn sale to US rival
data.description:
Fund giant Schroders has shocked the City with plans to sell itself to a US rival for £9.9bn, bringing its 67 years on the London stock market to an end.

The British money manager, which is one of the most recognised names in the City, has struck a merger deal with Nuveen, part of the Teachers Insurance and Annuity Association of America, a retirement savings group.

The proposed takeover will create one of the world’s largest asset managers with $2.5tn (£1.8tn) of assets, while ending Schroders’ independence after more than 200 years and dealing another blow to the ailing London Stock Exchange.

Richard Oldfield, Schroders’ chief executive, told The Telegraph the deal would “create the most powerful titan in active and wealth management globally”.

The deal is worth 612p per share, representing a 34pc premium to Schroders’ closing share price on Wednesday evening.

The agreement followed weeks of talks but came as a shock to investors and the City. Shares in the company surged around 28pc on the FTSE 100 to 587.5p.

The takeover of one of the largest UK-listed asset managers triggered speculation that rivals could also be vulnerable to a bid. Shares in Aberdeen Group rallied 1.7pc and Man Group rose 2.9pc in the wake of the news.

Schroder’s sale will also reignite concerns about the health of the London stock market and the broader City.

Dame Elizabeth Corley, Schroders’ chairman, insisted that despite quitting the stock market, London would “remain at the heart of this enlarged business”, serving as the combined group’s non-US headquarters.

Bosses also insisted that they did not intend to make any “material reductions” to the company’s headcount after the takeover, which is expected to close by the end of the year.

Mr Oldfield said: “If anyone thinks this is a step back from the UK or a step back from London, they haven’t really read the details of the deal. If we increase growth, London does even better than it would have done on a standalone basis.”

Schroders and Nuveen are expected to continue as standalone businesses.

Founded in 1804 by John Henry Schroder, the Schroders family still control around 44pc of the business, meaning they will make at least £4bn from the takeover.

Leonie Schroder and her cousin, Claire Fitzalan Howard, descendants of the founder, serve on the board.

Nuveen, founded in 1898 by John Nuveen, is headquartered in Chicago and currently manages $1.4tn in assets, with a heavy focus on the US market.

Mr Oldfield said the combination would “put us in the driving seat” in “an industry that is changing and consolidating quite rapidly”.

He added, “If we were on our own, we would be less able to do that.”

William Huffman, the chief executive of Nuveen, added, “This transaction is about unlocking new growth opportunities for wealth and institutional investors around the world by giving our leading, differentiated public-to-private platform a broader global presence.”

While Schroders has been a fixture of the FTSE 100 for decades, its share price growth has stalled in recent years.

Last April, its valuation fell to its lowest point in a decade as its share price hit 302p.

Its struggles have largely stemmed from savers turning towards cheaper passive funds, rather than paying a premium for Schroder’s market expertise.

The money manager has struggled to move beyond its roots in stock picking as more ruthless US fund giants such as BlackRock and Blackstone have embraced profitable alternative markets, such as private credit.

Despite a mergers-and-acquisitions spree over the years in which it bought up smaller alternative managers focused on private capital, Schroders has struggled to turn its investments into shareholder returns.

A fund manager at a rival asset manager said it was “no surprise” to see an overseas bidder swoop, given Schroders’ strong brand and positive transformation plan.

“It is yet another validation of the attractive value on offer in the UK equity market. Many strong franchises in the UK are trading at a discount to intrinsic value and as a result are attracting interest from corporate and private capital acquirers,” they said.

Some analysts suggest the offer undervalued Schroders. In a note titled “Too cheap but what can you do?”, Rae Maile, an analyst at Panmure Liberum, described the price as “only a touch ahead of where we might have been pitching a new target price in an independent world”.

He added: “With the offer being recommended the Family has clearly decided to move on, but the rest of us will be poorer for it.”

Under Mr Oldfield, who was appointed in November 2024, Schroders has undertaken a steep cost-cutting programme, looking to shave £150m off its bottom line.

Schroders’ exit from the London Stock Exchange will represent a significant blow to the market, with the City stalwart joining a growing exodus of FTSE giants.

Nuveen said that if it chose to relist the business in the future, it would look to dual-list the company on the London market and another exchange. However, there was no commitment that London would be the main venue for such a listing.

The deal will be funded by cash and £3bn of debt.
